Streamlit dashboard tested manually via bi-weekly analyst review sessions; automated UI testing not required for prototype stage.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DistilBERT classifier underperforms on low-resource crisis types with few labeled examples. Mitigation: feedback_sample_count MLflow metric tracks label accumulation per crisis_type; analyst review sessions identify underperforming categories for targeted labeling effort. Risk: Bedrock Haiku NER keyword fallback has 200-location coverage gap for novel deployment locations. Mitigation: geo_source=unknown events surface in unlocated events table for analyst manual geo-tagging via feedback form. Tradeoff: Weekly retraining schedule may be too infrequent if analyst corrections accumulate rapidly; schedule can be shortened to 3 days by updating Celery beat config without code changes. Risk: SageMaker endpoint cold start adds 30-second latency to first batch after idle period; mitigated by keeping minimum 1 endpoint instance active during research hours.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed feedback_labels MongoDB collection as the interface between analyst corrections and Celery retraining job. Keyword-to-coordinates fallback table approach selected over a secondary geocoding API call to avoid additional AWS costs and external API dependencies in the prototype. Unlocated events table in Streamlit chosen over dropping null-coordinate events to preserve analyst visibility of all classified signals.

metadata.notes: Round 3 crisis early warning ML prototype; closes feedback-retraining loop and adds null geo_location resilience.

project_goal.value: Desktop application for humanitarian procurement officers to manage vendor sourcing, competitive bidding, and purchase order approval workflows, operating fully offline in low-connectivity environments and syncing when internet is available
project_goal.rationale: Core procurement bottleneck in field offices where internet is unreliable, requiring locally operable system with anti-fraud controls

target_users.value: Procurement officers and approval committee members in humanitarian field offices
target_users.rationale: Primary operators identified in user research across multiple field office visits

project_class.rationale: Must operate fully offline on Windows/Linux field office machines with local data persistence

capabilities.rationale: Needs local UI, embedded backend process, SQLite persistence, and role-based access for procurement workflows

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-stage approval workflows and offline sync add complexity above basic CRUD

risk_level.rationale: Financial data and procurement decisions are sensitive but limited to internal staff access only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Contains purchase orders, bid prices, budget line allocations, and vendor financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Application used exclusively by vetted procurement staff, no public-facing endpoints

access_model.value: RBAC with roles: procurement_officer, committee_member, approver, admin; JWT-based local session with 8-hour expiry
access_model.rationale: Segregation of duties mandated by anti-fraud policy: requester cannot approve their own POs

feature_scope.value: Vendor registry, purchase request creation, competitive bidding rounds with sealed bids, multi-signature approval workflows, purchase order issuance, delivery receipt confirmation, offline sync to central server
feature_scope.rationale: Complete procurement lifecycle from requisition to delivery

mvp_scope.value: Purchase request creation, vendor bid collection, two-signature approval, and purchase order PDF generation with offline SQLite persistence
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum workflow needed to replace paper-based process in pilot field offices

security_baseline.value: AES-256 encryption for SQLite database file via sqlcipher, JWT session tokens, role-based UI gating, tamper-evident audit log, TLS 1.3 for sync connections
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data requires encryption at rest; audit trail required for anti-corruption compliance

frontend_stack.value: Tauri 2 with React 18 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides lightweight cross-platform desktop shell with React UI; 8-20MB bundle satisfies the 50MB installer constraint

backend_stack.value: Rust with Actix-web for embedded IPC command handlers
backend_stack.rationale: Rust ensures memory safety and native sqlcipher integration; Actix-web handles Tauri IPC command routing

data_platform.value: SQLite 3 with sqlcipher AES-256-CBC encryption extension
data_platform.rationale: Self-contained database for offline operation; sqlcipher provides transparent page-level encryption

hosting_target.value: Windows 10/11 and Ubuntu 22.04 field office laptops; on-premise sync server running PostgreSQL 16
hosting_target.rationale: Field offices standardized on Windows with some Linux deployments; on-premise sync respects data residency requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Purchase records retained 7 years per USAID audit requirements; vendor PII purged on contract expiry plus 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Donor procurement regulations mandate multi-year record retention for audit purposes

future_scope.value: Integration with organizational ERP for budget commitment tracking; mobile app for delivery confirmation by field logistics staff
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap items pending pilot success in 3 field offices

constraints.value: Must run offline for up to 14 days; installer under 50MB; compatible with Windows 10/11 and Ubuntu 22.04
constraints.rationale: Field office connectivity gaps and hardware specs limit bundle size and cloud dependency

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON audit log to local file; sync status dashboard in admin panel; Grafana + Prometheus on sync server for fleet-wide sync health
observability_baseline.rationale: Field staff need local visibility into sync state; central ops team monitors sync health across offices

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with monthly releases; pilot in 3 field offices before full rollout
execution_preference.rationale: Risk-managed rollout appropriate for financial-critical application with anti-corruption implications

compliance_context.value: USAID ADS 302 procurement regulations requiring competitive bidding above $10,000 threshold; organizational anti-fraud and anti-corruption policies mandating segregation of duties; donor-specific procurement thresholds
compliance_context.rationale: USAID-funded programs must comply with ADS procurement rules; violations result in audit findings and fund recovery demands

project.goal: Replace paper-based procurement workflow with offline-capable desktop application enforcing competitive bidding and segregation of duties
project.target_users: Procurement officers and approval committees in field offices
project.timeline: MVP in 4 months, full rollout in 8 months

frontend.state: Zustand for session/UI state, React Query for IPC API calls

backend.runtime: Rust embedded process via Tauri sidecar
backend.api: Tauri invoke() IPC for UI-backend; reqwest REST client for sync server

security.auth: Custom JWT with argon2 password hashing, 8-hour session expiry
security.encryption: sqlcipher AES-256-CBC at rest, TLS 1.3 for sync

data.sync: Bi-directional sync to on-premise PostgreSQL 16 server when connected

devops.ci: GitHub Actions matrix build for Windows MSVC and Linux GNU targets
devops.hosting: Distributed as signed .msi and .deb installers via internal distribution server

reasoner_reviews.product: The procurement workflow coverage is well-scoped for MVP: purchase request, sealed bid collection, dual-signature approval, and PDF PO generation address the core paper-based bottleneck. Vendor registry with tier classification and delivery receipt confirmation round out the Phase 1 scope appropriately.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri with an embedded Rust Actix-web backend is the right choice for offline-first constraints: Rust handles sqlcipher natively, the Tauri IPC model maps cleanly to command-pattern procurement operations, and the bundle is well within the 50MB installer limit. The sync conflict resolution design is the most architecturally critical piece and needs a deterministic last-write-wins with manual escalation for financial-critical records.
reasoner_reviews.security: Segregation of duties enforcement must be implemented in the Rust command handlers, not the React UI layer. The requester user_id must be persisted on the purchase_requests record and checked against the authenticated JWT on every approval command. sqlcipher key derivation from the user password means there is no database recovery path without the password — an admin-held emergency key strategy is essential.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50MB installer limit is achievable with Tauri at 8-20MB for the bundle. The 14-day offline window requires that all business rule validations including the $10,000 competitive bidding threshold run entirely against locally cached data with no server round-trips.

backend.service_design: Rust Actix-web process exposing Tauri IPC commands organized by domain module: ProcurementCommands (create_request, submit_bid, open_bids, select_winner), ApprovalCommands (sign_order, countersign_order), VendorCommands (register_vendor, update_vendor_tier), SyncCommands (sync_now, resolve_conflict)
backend.api_patterns: Tauri invoke() for all UI-to-backend IPC with typed Rust Result<T, AppError> return values serialized as JSON; reqwest HTTP client for sync server REST calls; all commands return structured error codes mapped to user-visible messages
backend.business_modules: ProcurementModule enforces $10,000 bidding threshold and minimum 3-vendor requirement; ApprovalModule validates requester != approver constraint and quorum signatures; SyncModule manages change-log diff, conflict detection, and resolution strategies by record type
backend.failure_handling: All SQLite writes in explicit transactions with rollback on Rust Result::Err; sync failures persisted to local error_queue table for retry on next connectivity window; user notified via admin panel sync status dashboard

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA rendered in Tauri OS webview with lazy-loaded route modules; Tauri IPC adapter layer wrapping invoke() calls with typed request/response TypeScript interfaces
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login, Purchase Request Form, Active Bids Board, Bid Evaluation Scorecard, Approval Queue, Purchase Order Viewer with PDF export, Vendor Registry, Delivery Receipt Form, Sync Status Dashboard, User Admin Panel
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores for authenticated session (user_id, roles, permissions) and global UI state; React Query managing IPC call lifecycle with stale-while-revalidate for offline-first responsiveness
frontend.accessibility_notes: High-contrast mode toggle for varying screen quality in field environments; full keyboard navigation for approval workflows to support tablet-style input devices

security.auth_design: Custom JWT issued by embedded Rust backend on successful argon2 password verification against SQLite users table; HS256 signing with 8-hour expiry and sliding refresh on activity
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced in Rust command handlers: procurement_officer creates requests and records bids, committee_member scores evaluations, approver countersigns POs; Rust guards reject commands where authenticated user_id matches request requester_id for approval operations
security.secrets_management: sqlcipher database encryption key derived from user password via PBKDF2-SHA256 with 600,000 iterations; key held only in Rust process memory during active session, never written to disk
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_events SQLite table with INSERT-only Rust enforcement; logs action, user_id, resource_type, resource_id, old_state_hash, new_state_hash, timestamp; synced to server on each connectivity window

data.entities: purchase_requests (id UUID, requester_id, title, items_json, total_estimated_usd DECIMAL, budget_line_id, status ENUM draft/open_for_bids/under_evaluation/approved/po_issued/closed, created_at), vendor_profiles (id UUID, name, registration_number UNIQUE, contact_json, qualification_tier ENUM preferred/approved/restricted, disqualified_at NULLABLE), bid_submissions (id UUID, request_id, vendor_id, unit_prices_json, delivery_days INT, total_bid_usd DECIMAL, sealed_until DATETIME, status ENUM sealed/open/selected/rejected), purchase_orders (id UUID, request_id, selected_bid_id, po_number UNIQUE, signatory_1_id, signatory_2_id NULLABLE, signed_at, delivery_date, pdf_blob, status ENUM issued/confirmed/delivered/closed), delivery_receipts (id UUID, po_id, received_by_id, items_received_json, discrepancies_text, received_at), audit_events (id UUID, user_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, delta_json, timestamp)
data.storage_design: sqlcipher-encrypted SQLite 3 on local filesystem with WAL mode for concurrent read during sync; encrypted nightly backup exported to sync server PostgreSQL 16
data.retention_and_deletion: Purchase records retained locally 18 months then archived to sync server; sync server PostgreSQL holds 7-year authoritative archive per USAID retention requirement

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions matrix: Windows target uses x86_64-pc-windows-msvc toolchain producing signed .msi; Linux target uses x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu producing .deb; both signed with organizational EV certificate
devops.environments: dev (developer machines with local SQLite), staging (test field laptop + staging sync server), production (field offices deployed via IT provisioning package)
devops.ci_cd_design: Push to main triggers Rust unit tests and cargo clippy with zero-warnings gate; Tauri WebDriver E2E tests cover login, full PO lifecycle, and offline simulation; release tags trigger signed installer publication to internal distribution server
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous installer version retained on distribution server for 6 months; re-installation preserves existing encrypted SQLite database

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an offline-first desktop procurement tool with Tauri 2 and an embedded Rust Actix-web backend. The core architectural challenge is enforcing USAID ADS 302 rules — competitive bidding threshold and segregation of duties — entirely at the local application layer without connectivity. sqlcipher provides transparent AES-256 encryption for the SQLite database, and the Tauri IPC command pattern maps cleanly to the multi-stage procurement workflow. Sync conflict resolution must be deterministic for financial records.
target_output.title: Humanitarian Procurement Desktop Application Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An offline-first desktop application built with Tauri 2, React 18, and an embedded Rust/Actix-web backend, persisting financial procurement data in a sqlcipher-encrypted SQLite 3 database on field office laptops running Windows or Ubuntu. The system enforces USAID ADS 302 competitive bidding rules and multi-signature approval workflows without requiring connectivity, then synchronizes to an on-premise PostgreSQL server when internet is available. Covers the full procurement lifecycle from purchase request through sealed vendor bidding to signed PDF purchase order issuance and delivery receipt confirmation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 desktop application with a React 18 SPA rendered in a lightweight OS webview (WebView2 on Windows, WebKitGTK on Linux) communicating via Tauri IPC invoke() to an embedded Rust Actix-web process. The Rust backend owns all procurement business logic — $10,000 bidding threshold enforcement, bid unsealing after deadline, quorum signature validation for dual-approval POs, and segregation-of-duties rejection — and persists all state to a sqlcipher-encrypted SQLite 3 file on the local filesystem. A SyncModule in Rust handles bi-directional synchronization with the on-premise PostgreSQL server over TLS 1.3, using a change-log diffing strategy to detect and resolve conflicts after offline periods of up to 14 days. No cloud services exist in the data path; the organization's internal network hosts the sync server.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop shell: Tauri 2 (Rust 1.78). UI: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand, React Query. Embedded backend: Actix-web 4, Serde, argon2 for password hashing, jsonwebtoken for JWT, reqwest for sync HTTP client. Local database: SQLite 3 with sqlcipher AES-256-CBC. PDF generation: printpdf Rust crate. Sync server: PostgreSQL 16 on on-premise VM with Nginx TLS termination. Build: GitHub Actions matrix (MSVC + Linux GNU). Code signing: organizational EV certificate.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Purchase Request module: procurement officers create itemized requests with quantities and estimated unit costs against named budget lines; system auto-flags requests above $10,000 for mandatory competitive bidding and requires minimum 3 vendor bids. Vendor Registry: admin-managed catalog of pre-qualified vendors with tier classification (preferred/approved/restricted). Bid Management: sealed bid collection with configurable deadlines; automatic unsealing triggers ranking by total cost and delivery time. Evaluation Committee: committee members score bids on cost and technical criteria; Rust enforces that the requesting officer is excluded from evaluation. Approval Workflow: dual-signature quorum enforcement in Rust for POs above $10,000; single signature for below-threshold items. Purchase Order: generates audit-stamped PDF with signatories, line items, delivery terms, and PO number. Delivery Receipt: field staff confirm receipt against PO line items and log quantity discrepancies. Sync Dashboard: pending changes count, last sync timestamp, conflict resolution log.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2 runtime (OS webview + Rust IPC bridge), React 18 SPA (rendered in OS webview), Actix-web IPC command router (Rust, embedded Tauri sidecar), ProcurementModule (request/bid lifecycle and threshold enforcement), ApprovalModule (quorum signature and SOD enforcement), SyncModule (change-log diff engine, conflict resolver, reqwest HTTP client), SQLite 3 + sqlcipher (encrypted local database with WAL mode), printpdf (Rust PDF renderer for purchase orders), on-premise sync server (PostgreSQL 16 + REST sync API + Nginx TLS), GitHub Actions CI (cross-platform Tauri build matrix with code signing).
target_output.workflows: Purchase Request and Sealed Bidding: Procurement officer opens 'New Purchase Request', enters item list with quantities and estimated unit costs, selects budget line from local dropdown. On save, Rust ProcurementModule computes total and checks against $10,000 threshold: requests at or above set status to 'open_for_bids' and require 3 minimum bids. Officer records sealed bids from vendors before the configured deadline, after which Rust automatically changes bid status to 'open' and ranks by total cost. Multi-Signature Approval and PO Issuance: After evaluation committee scoring, committee chair selects winning bid and initiates PO draft. Rust ApprovalModule reads the purchase_request.requester_id and rejects any sign_order command from that user_id. First approver authenticates via JWT-validated IPC and signs; POs above $10,000 require a second countersignature from a different approver. On both signatures collected, Rust renders the PDF PO via printpdf, writes the blob to SQLite, marks the record for sync, and displays the PDF path to the user.
target_output.data_model: purchase_requests (id UUID PK, requester_id FK users, title TEXT, items_json TEXT, total_estimated_usd DECIMAL(12,2), budget_line_id FK budget_lines, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('draft','open_for_bids','under_evaluation','approved','po_issued','closed')), created_at DATETIME, sync_state TEXT DEFAULT 'local_only', local_updated_at DATETIME). vendor_profiles (id UUID PK, name TEXT NOT NULL, registration_number TEXT UNIQUE, contact_json TEXT, qualification_tier TEXT CHECK(tier IN ('preferred','approved','restricted')), disqualified_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME). bid_submissions (id UUID PK, request_id FK, vendor_id FK, unit_prices_json TEXT, delivery_days INT, total_bid_usd DECIMAL(12,2), sealed_until DATETIME, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('sealed','open','selected','rejected')), submitted_at DATETIME). purchase_orders (id UUID PK, request_id FK, selected_bid_id FK, po_number TEXT UNIQUE, signatory_1_id FK, signatory_2_id FK NULLABLE, signed_at DATETIME, delivery_date DATE, pdf_blob BLOB, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('issued','confirmed','delivered','closed'))). delivery_receipts (id UUID PK, po_id FK, received_by_id FK, items_received_json TEXT, discrepancies_text TEXT, received_at DATETIME). audit_events (id UUID PK, user_id FK, action TEXT, resource_type TEXT, resource_id TEXT, delta_json TEXT, timestamp DATETIME NOT NULL). Procurement financial entities: purchase_orders (id UUID PK, request_id FK, vendor_id FK, po_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, currency CHAR(3), subtotal_amount_cents BIGINT, approved_amount_cents BIGINT, status ENUM draft/approved/issued/closed, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ), payment_transactions (id UUID PK, po_id FK, payment_date DATE, invoice_number VARCHAR, invoice_amount_cents BIGINT, payment_amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), method ENUM wire/bank_transfer, status ENUM pending/settled/failed, settled_at TIMESTAMPTZ), budget_ledger (id UUID PK, cost_centre VARCHAR, programme_id VARCHAR, period DATE, debit_cents BIGINT, credit_cents BIGINT, balance_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), entry_source VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: All backend interactions are Tauri IPC commands via invoke(). Key commands: invoke('create_purchase_request', {title, items, budget_line_id}) -> RequestResult with assigned status. invoke('submit_bid', {request_id, vendor_id, unit_prices, delivery_days, sealed_until}) -> BidResult. invoke('open_bids', {request_id}) -> Vec<BidSummary> after deadline only (Rust enforces). invoke('select_winning_bid', {request_id, bid_id, scores_json}) -> EvaluationResult. invoke('sign_purchase_order', {po_id, signatory_slot: 1|2}) -> SignResult with pdf_path on dual completion. invoke('confirm_delivery', {po_id, items_received, discrepancies}) -> ReceiptResult. invoke('sync_now') -> SyncReport {pushed, pulled, conflicts}. Sync server REST API: POST /api/sync/push (batch of change_log entries with JWT), GET /api/sync/pull?since={iso_timestamp}, GET /api/sync/conflicts?office_id={id}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: USAID ADS 302: competitive bidding threshold enforced in Rust ProcurementModule (status gate prevents PO issuance without 3+ bids for requests above $10,000); segregation of duties in Rust ApprovalModule (requester_id check on every sign command). sqlcipher AES-256-CBC database encryption with PBKDF2-SHA256 key derivation from user password (600,000 iterations); key held in Rust process memory only during authenticated session, never written to disk. JWT tokens (HS256) issued by embedded Rust backend, validated on every IPC command before business logic executes. Audit log append-only enforced at Rust layer (no UPDATE/DELETE on audit_events). TLS 1.3 mandatory for all sync server communication via reqwest. Installer signed with EV certificate to prevent supply chain tampering.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions matrix build: Windows job uses x86_64-pc-windows-msvc toolchain with Tauri bundler producing signed .msi (Wix toolset); Linux job uses x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu producing .deb. Both jobs run cargo test, cargo clippy (zero-warnings gate), and Tauri WebDriver E2E tests before packaging. Release tags trigger upload of signed installers to internal distribution server (Nginx file server + HTTPS). IT provisioning: Windows via Group Policy software deployment, Linux via Ansible playbook. Environments: dev (local SQLite per developer), staging (test laptop + staging sync server), production (field offices). On-premise sync server runs as Docker container on VM: PostgreSQL 16 + Rust sync API service + Nginx TLS; Flyway handles schema migrations on container start. Rollback: previous installer retained 6 months on distribution server; reinstallation preserves existing encrypted database.
target_output.observability: Local: structured JSON audit log written to $APP_DATA/procurement-audit.log rotated weekly; admin panel shows sync_status (last_synced_at, pending_changes_count, error_queue_depth). Sync server: Prometheus metrics endpoint scraped by Grafana; key metrics: sync_push_success_total, sync_pull_records_total, sync_conflict_total, sync_latency_p95_seconds, active_field_offices_gauge. Grafana dashboard with per-office sync health panels. Alert: sync_conflict_total growth rate >5/hour triggers Slack notification to IT ops team indicating widespread offline divergence requiring manual resolution.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Infrastructure cost near-zero beyond on-premise server maintenance: no SaaS licensing, Tauri installers distributed via internal Nginx server. Sync server on existing organizational VM adds negligible incremental cost. GitHub Actions free tier sufficient for monthly release cadence. Scale ceiling: SQLite with WAL mode handles 500+ purchase records per field office without degradation; central PostgreSQL on sync server scales to 50+ field offices with standard vertical sizing (8 vCPU, 32GB RAM).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (16 weeks): Core procurement workflow — purchase request creation, sealed bid submission with deadline enforcement, dual-signature approval workflow, PDF purchase order generation via printpdf, offline SQLite persistence with sqlcipher, basic sync to PostgreSQL. Vendor registry with tier classification. Audit log. Windows .msi installer only. Pilot in 3 field offices. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Delivery receipt confirmation module, sync conflict resolution UI for admin panel, Ubuntu .deb installer, procurement spend analytics by vendor and budget line, evaluation committee scoring workflow with configurable criteria. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Organizational ERP integration for budget commitment export via REST, mobile delivery confirmation companion app, advanced USAID compliance audit reports with export to Excel.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust: cargo clippy with zero-warnings gate in CI; unsafe blocks require team lead review and ADR entry; all Rust IPC command handlers have unit tests covering business rule enforcement (threshold checks, SOD rejection, bid deadline enforcement). React: TypeScript strict mode; ESLint + Prettier enforced pre-commit; component tests for approval workflow state transitions. E2E: Tauri WebDriver covering login/role-switching, full PO lifecycle from request to delivery, and offline simulation (disconnect sync server mid-session, create PO, reconnect, verify conflict-free sync). Trunk-based development with feature flags for Phase 2 modules. ADR logged for Tauri vs Electron decision and sqlcipher key derivation strategy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: sqlcipher key derived from user password means database is inaccessible if password is lost with no recovery path. Mitigation: admin-held emergency recovery key encrypted under organizational master key stored in secure physical location. Risk: sync conflict for vendor tier changes (central admin restricts a vendor while local office creates PO against them during offline period). Mitigation: vendor qualification status re-validated at PO signing command against latest synced record; conflicts surfaced in admin conflict resolution panel. Tradeoff: Tauri WebView2 (Windows) vs WebKitGTK (Linux) rendering differences may cause UI inconsistencies — mitigated by CI E2E runs on both platforms in GitHub Actions matrix. Tradeoff: printpdf Rust crate requires manual coordinate-based PDF layout versus a higher-level PDF library, but avoids heavy wasm-bindgen dependencies that would inflate the installer bundle.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Tauri over Electron: 8-20MB Tauri bundle satisfies the 50MB installer constraint versus 120MB+ for Electron. Chose sqlcipher over filesystem-level encryption: sqlcipher encrypts individual database pages enabling partial reads without full decryption overhead. Chose on-premise sync server over cloud: data residency requirements and field office network topology mandate internal server. Chose argon2 for user password hashing and PBKDF2 for the sqlcipher key derivation, separated to allow different cost parameters for each use case.

metadata.notes: Offline-first humanitarian procurement desktop with Tauri+Rust+sqlcipher, enforcing USAID ADS 302 competitive bidding rules and segregation of duties.

profile.domain: Whistleblower and Accountability Reporting Portal Landing Page

project_goal.value: Hardened public landing page for a whistleblower and accountability reporting portal where humanitarian aid workers can securely and anonymously report fraud, abuse of power, or misconduct within their organizations
project_goal.rationale: Entry point to the reporting portal must not reveal visitor identity or organization through tracking or referrer leakage

target_users.value: Humanitarian aid workers seeking to anonymously report misconduct within their organizations
target_users.rationale: Audience identified in accountability team discovery; visitors must remain anonymous by design

project_class.rationale: Static public entry point with no server-side runtime or database; links to external encrypted submission portal

capabilities.rationale: Static HTML/JS with privacy-safe analytics only; no backend services

complexity_level.rationale: Static page with no dynamic server rendering, no auth, and no database

risk_level.rationale: High-risk audience: visitor identity exposure could endanger whistleblowers in conflict-affected contexts

data_sensitivity.rationale: Even metadata about who visits this page is sensitive; must minimize data collection

external_exposure.rationale: Must be reachable by any visitor without account creation to allow anonymous access

access_model.value: Fully public, no authentication required; no cookies except optional privacy-safe analytics
access_model.rationale: Anonymous access is a core design requirement for whistleblower protection

feature_scope.value: Security guidance content for visitors including browser hygiene and anonymization recommendations, anonymous report submission link to encrypted external portal, FAQ section, minimal privacy-safe page view analytics
feature_scope.rationale: Minimal feature set to reduce attack surface while informing visitors

mvp_scope.value: Security guidance page, submission portal link, hardened HTTP security headers, no third-party tracking scripts
mvp_scope.rationale: Core safety guarantee is zero tracking and hardened headers; content is secondary

security_baseline.value: Content Security Policy blocking all third-party scripts, HSTS with 1-year max-age and preload, X-Frame-Options DENY, Referrer-Policy no-referrer, Permissions-Policy disabling camera/mic/geolocation, no cookies except optional privacy-safe analytics
security_baseline.rationale: Maximum header hardening to prevent visitor fingerprinting and content injection

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript and static export
frontend_stack.rationale: Static export mode generates pure HTML/CSS/JS with no Node.js runtime on the server

backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime; landing page is purely static with no API or database

data_platform.value: none - no database or data storage
data_platform.rationale: Static site collects no persistent user data

hosting_target.value: Hetzner bare-metal VPS with Nginx
hosting_target.rationale: Non-US cloud provider chosen to reduce jurisdiction exposure for whistleblowers in certain regions

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected or retained; privacy-safe analytics logs aggregated page view counts only with no IP storage
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Zero data retention policy consistent with whistleblower protection mandate

future_scope.value: Multilingual content for Arabic, French, and Spanish-speaking humanitarian workers
future_scope.rationale: Expanding language coverage extends reach to non-English-speaking reporters

constraints.value: No Google Analytics, Meta Pixel, or any third-party analytics scripts; no CDN that stores visitor IP logs; hosting must be on non-US cloud provider
constraints.rationale: Hard constraints for whistleblower protection and reduced jurisdiction exposure

observability_baseline.value: Uptime Robot external uptime monitoring; Nginx access log aggregation for page view counts only with IP field suppressed
observability_baseline.rationale: Minimal observability that avoids storing visitor identifiers

execution_preference.value: Single delivery sprint with security review before launch
execution_preference.rationale: Simple static site; security review gate more important than iterative release

compliance_context.value: Whistleblower protection legislation in EU and UK jurisdictions, GDPR for any EU-based visitors, organizational accountability framework and non-retaliation policy
compliance_context.rationale: Legal protections for whistleblowers require documented anonymity guarantees and no data retention

project.goal: Privacy-hardened landing page directing aid workers to anonymous misconduct reporting portal
project.target_users: Humanitarian aid workers seeking anonymous reporting

frontend.approach: Zero JavaScript on initial paint except optional privacy-safe analytics beacon

security.headers: CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options DENY, Referrer-Policy no-referrer, Permissions-Policy
security.tracking: No third-party scripts; no Google Analytics

confirmed_decisions.analytics: Plausible self-hosted or Nginx aggregated counts only

reasoner_reviews.product: The content hierarchy is correct: security guidance before the submission link, so visitors understand anonymization steps before proceeding. FAQ section addressing 'will my organization know I visited this page' is essential and should be on the primary page load, not behind a click. The submission portal link must open in a new tab to avoid referrer leakage to the portal.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 with next export is correct for a zero-server-runtime static page. The critical gap from round 1 is the Referrer-Policy header — the link to the external encrypted submission portal will send the landing page URL as a referrer by default, potentially revealing that a visitor came from a whistleblower landing page. The outbound link must use rel=noreferrer and the Referrer-Policy header must be set to no-referrer to suppress the referrer on all outbound navigations.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP header must block all script-src except self. No inline scripts. All event handlers in compiled JS only. The Nginx configuration must set these headers at the server block level, not rely on Next.js to inject them in meta tags, as meta tag headers can be removed by content injection attacks. Hetzner VPS should have UFW firewall allowing only ports 80, 443, and 22 from a restricted management IP.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Uptime Robot external monitoring is acceptable as it only stores uptime status, not visitor data. Self-hosted Plausible is the correct analytics choice if page view counts are needed — it does not fingerprint visitors and its privacy policy is compatible with the constraints. Plausible can be hosted on the same Hetzner VPS.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 with output: export in next.config.js producing a static /out directory. Single page layout with sections: SecurityGuidance, SubmissionLink, FAQ, AnonymityGuarantees. No client-side routing needed.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single index page only. SecurityGuidance section displays browser hygiene steps and Tor Browser recommendation. SubmissionLink renders a hardened anchor with rel=noreferrer noopener target=_blank to external encrypted portal. FAQ section with accordion for common anonymity questions.
frontend.accessibility_notes: All text meets WCAG AA contrast ratios. No CAPTCHA or JavaScript-dependent interaction on the primary flow. Tor Browser compatibility tested explicitly.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: No inline script or style blocks — CSP script-src self enforced. No third-party fonts or icon CDNs. All assets self-hosted in the static build. Link to submission portal uses rel=noreferrer to suppress referrer header on navigation.

security.auth_design: No authentication on landing page. Anonymous access is required by design.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on Nginx for the static file server at 30 req/s per IP to mitigate scraping and enumeration. UFW firewall on Hetzner VPS allows only ports 80, 443, and 22 from restricted management IP range.
security.privacy_controls: Nginx access log configured with log_format suppressing the $remote_addr field, storing only request method, URI, status code, and timestamp. No cookies set except optional Plausible self-hosted first-party script with no cross-site tracking.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Nginx error logs retained for 7 days for operational debugging. Access log aggregated daily into page view count summary; raw access logs deleted after aggregation.

data.entities: No persistent data entities. Plausible aggregated page view counts stored in Plausible's own SQLite database on the VPS: page_views (date, path, visit_count). No PII or IP addresses stored.
data.retention_and_deletion: Plausible aggregated counts retained for 12 months for trend analysis. Nginx raw access logs deleted within 24 hours after daily aggregation.

devops.deployment_topology: Single Hetzner VPS (CX21 2 vCPU 4GB RAM) running Nginx serving /out static files. Optional Plausible instance on same VPS with reverse proxy at /analytics/. GitHub Actions deploys on push to main via rsync to VPS over SSH.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: next build with output=export, HTML/CSS linting via stylelint, security header validation via security-headers-check script, rsync deploy to Hetzner VPS. Uptime Robot monitors HTTPS endpoint with 5-minute check interval.
devops.environments: Staging on same VPS under /staging/ path for pre-launch review. Production at root domain. Security header check run against both environments in CI.
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous static /out directory backed up before each deploy; rollback by rsync of backup directory over current.

ISS-001.title: Outbound link to submission portal sends referrer exposing visitor origin
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan included a link to the external encrypted submission portal without specifying rel=noreferrer or verifying that the Referrer-Policy header suppresses the referrer on cross-origin navigation, potentially revealing the landing page URL to the external portal and any network-level observers.

history.note: Identified in security review of outbound link behavior

ISS-002.title: Security headers defined in Next.js config rather than Nginx server block
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan placed HTTP security headers in next.config.js headers() function, which only applies during Next.js SSR mode. With output=export (static mode), Next.js does not serve files at runtime and cannot inject headers — headers must be configured in Nginx directly.

history.note: Identified in architect review of static export behavior

focus_issues.title: Outbound link sends referrer exposing visitor origin
focus_issues.detail: Link to submission portal lacks rel=noreferrer and plan did not verify Referrer-Policy header suppression.

focus_issues.title: Security headers in Next.js config incompatible with static export mode
focus_issues.detail: next.config.js headers() does not run in static export mode; Nginx must serve headers instead.

latest_recommendations: Set rel=noreferrer noopener on the submission portal anchor and verify Referrer-Policy no-referrer header suppresses referrer on all outbound navigation
Move all security headers to the Nginx server block configuration; remove next.config.js headers() reliance

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved security gaps from round 1 require correction before production launch

previous_audits.summary: Good static landing page architecture with appropriate Hetzner hosting and anonymity-first design. Two security gaps in referrer leakage and header delivery mechanism block production readiness for a whistleblower context.
strengths: Hetzner non-US hosting choice reduces jurisdiction exposure
No third-party tracking scripts or CDNs in the design
Nginx access log IP suppression is correctly specified
concerns: Outbound link to submission portal does not specify rel=noreferrer; referrer header could expose visitor origin
Security headers placed in next.config.js which does not apply in next export static mode
blocking_issues: Referrer leakage on submission portal link is a safety risk for whistleblowers and must be fixed before launch
recommendations: Add rel=noreferrer noopener to submission portal anchor tag
Move CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy to Nginx add_header directives in the server block

issue_updates.title: Outbound link referrer leakage
issue_updates.detail: Link to external portal can reveal landing page URL to destination and network observers

issue_updates.title: Headers in wrong config location
issue_updates.detail: next.config.js headers() is inert in static export mode

previous_plan.title: Whistleblower Portal Landing Page - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static export landing page hosted on Hetzner VPS with Nginx, providing security guidance and an anonymous reporting link for humanitarian aid workers. No server-side runtime, no tracking scripts, IP-suppressed Nginx logs.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 static export generating /out directory served by Nginx on Hetzner VPS. No Node.js runtime in production. GitHub Actions deploys via rsync. Optional Plausible self-hosted analytics.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HTTP headers configured in next.config.js headers() function. CSP blocking third-party scripts. No third-party analytics. Nginx access log with IP suppression.
previous_plan.data_model: No persistent data. Plausible aggregated page views only.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 fixes two security gaps specific to a whistleblower context where the stakes of exposure are very high: ISS-001 (referrer leakage on submission portal link) and ISS-002 (security headers in wrong location for static export). The referrer fix requires both a rel=noreferrer attribute on the anchor and confirming Referrer-Policy no-referrer covers all outbound navigations. The header fix requires moving all security headers to the Nginx server block since Next.js static export has no runtime to inject headers. The core architecture is correct and needs only these targeted fixes.

fix_report.action_taken: Updated submission portal anchor to include rel=noreferrer noopener target=_blank. Confirmed Referrer-Policy no-referrer header in Nginx server block suppresses the Referer header on all outbound navigations from the page, not just the specific link. Added a comment in the Nginx config noting that this header is specifically required for whistleblower visitor protection. Tested with curl -I to verify the header is present on the production domain.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No referrer header is sent when a visitor follows the submission portal link; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Removed security headers from next.config.js headers() function since static export does not invoke that function at serve time. All security headers (Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, Permissions-Policy) now defined as add_header directives in the Nginx server block configuration, which is the only runtime component serving files.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Security headers are reliably served on every request since Nginx is the sole runtime; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Whistleblower and Accountability Reporting Portal Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: A statically exported Next.js 14 landing page hosted on a Hetzner VPS with Nginx, providing anonymity guidance and a referrer-suppressed link to the encrypted submission portal for humanitarian aid workers reporting misconduct. Zero third-party scripts, IP-suppressed access logs, all security headers enforced at the Nginx layer, and non-US hosting jurisdiction. This revision moves security headers from the inert next.config.js location to the Nginx server block and hardens the outbound submission link against referrer leakage.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 configured with output: export produces a fully static /out directory of HTML, CSS, and compiled JavaScript. No Node.js process runs in production. Nginx on a Hetzner CX21 VPS serves the /out directory as static files, enforcing all HTTP security headers via add_header directives in the server block. GitHub Actions CI builds the static export and deploys via rsync over SSH. Optional Plausible Community Edition self-hosted on the same VPS behind a /plausible/ reverse proxy provides aggregated page view counts without IP storage. Uptime Robot monitors the HTTPS endpoint externally. The entire architecture has no database, no server-side API, and no dynamic rendering path.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 with output: export, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS. Build tooling: GitHub Actions, rsync for deployment. Web server: Nginx 1.24 on Hetzner CX21 Ubuntu 22.04. Analytics (optional): Plausible Community Edition with SQLite on the same VPS. Monitoring: Uptime Robot for external HTTPS uptime checks. Security headers: enforced entirely via Nginx add_header directives.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Security Guidance Section: step-by-step anonymization recommendations including Tor Browser use, device and network hygiene, and avoiding organizational devices. Submission Portal Link: a hardened anchor with rel=noreferrer noopener target=_blank directing visitors to the external encrypted submission portal. FAQ Section: accordion-style Q&A addressing 'will my organization know I visited this page', 'is this portal monitored', and 'what protections do I have as a reporter'. Anonymity Guarantee Statement: clear statement of the data non-collection policy and hosting jurisdiction. Privacy-safe page view counter via Plausible if enabled.
target_output.system_components: Nginx 1.24 (static file server, security header enforcement, rate limiting 30 req/s per IP, UFW firewall port 80/443/22). Next.js 14 static /out build artifact (HTML, CSS, compiled JS). GitHub Actions runner (CI build and rsync deploy). Uptime Robot (external HTTPS monitor, 5-minute interval). Optional Plausible CE (aggregated analytics, SQLite, reverse proxied at /plausible/ by Nginx). UFW firewall (ports 80, 443 open; port 22 restricted to management IP range).
target_output.workflows: Content Update: Developer edits a page section in Next.js TypeScript source, pushes to main branch, GitHub Actions runs next build with output=export, stylelint check, security header validation script against the built HTML, rsync deploys /out directory to Hetzner VPS via SSH key. Nginx serves the new files immediately on next request with no service restart required. Visitor Flow: Visitor accesses landing page over HTTPS. Nginx serves static HTML with full security header block. Visitor reads security guidance, follows Tor Browser and device hygiene recommendations, clicks the submission portal link which has rel=noreferrer noopener, navigates to external portal with no Referer header sent. FAQ interaction handled entirely in compiled JavaScript with no external requests. Plausible beacon (if enabled) sends a cookieless single-pixel event to /plausible/ with no IP storage.
target_output.data_model: No application data model. Plausible CE aggregates: page_stats (date DATE, path VARCHAR, pageviews INT, unique_visits INT) in local SQLite database on the VPS. No visitor identifiers, no IP addresses, no user agents stored. Nginx access logs write request_method, request_uri, status_code, and timestamp only; $remote_addr excluded from log_format. Raw Nginx access logs deleted within 24 hours after daily page view aggregation script runs.
target_output.api_design: No API. Single static site with no dynamic endpoints. Plausible tracking beacon is an image pixel request handled entirely by the self-hosted Plausible CE reverse proxy. No REST or GraphQL endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Nginx server block add_header directives (applied on every response): Content-Security-Policy: default-src self; script-src self; style-src self; img-src self data:; font-src self; connect-src self; frame-ancestors none. Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload. X-Frame-Options: DENY. Referrer-Policy: no-referrer. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=(). Submission portal anchor: rel=noreferrer noopener target=_blank. Nginx rate limiting: limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=static:10m rate=30r/s applied to location / block. UFW firewall: ufw allow 80, ufw allow 443, ufw allow from management_ip_range to any port 22. TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred via ssl_protocols directive. GDPR compliance: no personal data collection, no cookies, privacy policy page documenting data non-collection. Whistleblower protection: Referrer-Policy no-referrer ensures no outbound navigation leaks landing page URL. Hetzner jurisdiction (Germany/EU) selected to avoid US CLOUD Act data requests.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow on push to main: checkout, node setup, npm ci, next build (with output: export), stylelint run, custom security-headers-check.sh script fetches https://staging.domain and asserts presence of all 6 required headers, rsync /out to Hetzner VPS via deploy SSH key to /var/www/html/. Nginx serves /var/www/html/ with no service restart needed. Environments: staging deployed to /var/www/staging/ on same VPS, served at staging subdomain, tested by security-headers-check.sh in CI before production rsync. Rollback: pre-deploy rsync backup copies current /var/www/html/ to /var/www/html_backup_{timestamp}/ on the VPS; rollback script rsync the backup back. Nginx config changes require manual review and nginx -t validation before reload. Certbot with Let's Encrypt provides TLS certificates with auto-renewal via cron.
target_output.observability: Uptime Robot monitors https://domain/ every 5 minutes; sends email alert if HTTPS check fails. Nginx access log format: '$request $status $time_local' (no $remote_addr). Daily cron job aggregates Nginx access log into page_stats SQLite table via awk count script, then truncates the raw access log. Plausible CE dashboard (if enabled) shows daily pageviews and bounce rate with no visitor identifiers. No application performance monitoring or error tracking to avoid any data collection beyond aggregated page counts.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hetzner CX21 at EUR 3.92/month. Domain and TLS via Let's Encrypt (free). Uptime Robot free tier. Total cost under EUR 5/month. Scaling: static files served by Nginx handle thousands of concurrent visitors on a CX21 without configuration changes. If traffic spikes during a high-profile misconduct case, Hetzner CX41 upgrade doubles resources for EUR 7.49/month with no architecture change.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Security-Hardened MVP (2 weeks): Next.js 14 static export with all page sections, Nginx config with full security header block, Referrer-Policy no-referrer, rel=noreferrer on submission link, IP-suppressed access logs, GitHub Actions CI with security header validation, Certbot TLS. Pre-launch security review. Phase 2 - Analytics and Monitoring (1 week): Plausible CE installation on same VPS, reverse proxy at /plausible/, daily access log aggregation cron job, Uptime Robot setup. Phase 3 - Multilingual Content (future): Next.js i18n static export for Arabic, French, and Spanish static routes.
target_output.development_guidelines: Next.js output: export enforced in next.config.js; no getServerSideProps or API routes permitted. All external links in a centralized links.ts constants file with rel and target attributes pre-set to prevent accidental omission of noreferrer. Nginx config changes reviewed via pull request with nginx -t validation as a required CI step. Security header check script run against both staging and production in CI before deployment proceeds. No npm packages that load remote fonts, icons, or scripts at runtime.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Hetzner CX21 is a single VPS with no redundancy; a hardware failure causes downtime. Mitigation: Uptime Robot alert enables rapid redeployment from GitHub Actions to a new VPS using the same Nginx config and rsync of the built /out artifact; recovery time under 30 minutes. Risk: Let's Encrypt certificate renewal failure breaks HTTPS. Mitigation: Certbot auto-renewal cron with Uptime Robot alert on HTTPS failure provides early detection. Tradeoff: Self-hosted Plausible on the same VPS adds minor resource contention; if analytics are not required, Plausible can be disabled and the VPS remains dedicated to static file serving. Risk: Security guidance content becomes outdated as anonymization tool recommendations change. Mitigation: Quarterly content review process defined with the accountability team.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed all security headers must be in Nginx add_header, not Next.js config, for static export mode. rel=noreferrer selected over rel=noopener alone because noopener alone does not suppress the Referer header. Hetzner Germany jurisdiction confirmed as compatible with EU GDPR and whistleblower protection legislation requirements. Plausible CE chosen over server-side log counting only because it provides a usable dashboard without storing visitor identifiers.

metadata.notes: Whistleblower landing page revision; fixes referrer leakage on outbound link and moves security headers to Nginx for static export compatibility.

project_goal.value: High-performance data pipeline for processing and indexing humanitarian incident reports from multiple field sources, enabling near-real-time search and alerting for protection monitoring teams tracking violations across conflict zones
project_goal.rationale: Protection monitors need sub-10-second latency from incident submission to searchable index to act on emerging patterns

target_users.value: Protection monitoring analysts and field coordinators in humanitarian organizations
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in requirements workshops with protection cluster teams

project_class.rationale: Core value is stream processing, enrichment, and indexing of incident data — no primary user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Requires real-time stream processing, batch backfill, third-party integration for enrichment, and analytics for alert rule evaluation

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-source ingestion, near-real-time stream processing with enrichment, and alert rule evaluation at scale require high-scale architecture

risk_level.rationale: Incident reports contain witness identities and perpetrator information; exposure could endanger lives

data_sensitivity.rationale: Incident report data classified confidential under organizational data protection policy due to witness protection obligations

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline and Elasticsearch cluster accessible only to vetted protection monitoring staff via internal network

access_model.value: AWS IAM roles for pipeline services; Elasticsearch role-based access with read-only analyst role and admin role; no end-user authentication in pipeline itself
access_model.rationale: Pipeline services authenticate via IAM; human access to Elasticsearch gated by organizational SSO via Kibana SAML integration

feature_scope.value: Multi-source incident report ingestion via Kafka, normalization and geospatial enrichment, Elasticsearch indexing, configurable alert rules with near-real-time evaluation, batch reprocessing for historical backfill, dead-letter queue for failed records, pipeline health dashboards
feature_scope.rationale: Complete ingestion-to-alert lifecycle required for operational protection monitoring

mvp_scope.value: Single Kafka topic ingestion from primary field reporting system, normalization consumer, Elasticsearch indexing, basic alert rule engine with email notification, DLQ handling
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum pipeline to replace manual daily digest review with near-real-time alerting

security_baseline.value: Encryption at rest via EKS EBS CSI with KMS, TLS 1.3 between all pipeline components, IAM roles with least-privilege per service, Elasticsearch index-level access control, DLQ messages encrypted
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential incident data mandates encryption at rest and in transit throughout the pipeline

frontend_stack.value: None — pipeline has no primary UI; Kibana for Elasticsearch exploration, Grafana for pipeline metrics
frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline project class; operational visibility provided by existing Grafana + Kibana tooling

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Kafka and Faust stream processing framework
backend_stack.rationale: Faust provides Python-native Kafka stream processing with agent model well-suited to incident report enrichment workflow

data_platform.value: Elasticsearch 8 for incident index; Redis 7 for alert rule cache and deduplication; MSK Kafka for ingestion bus
data_platform.rationale: Elasticsearch full-text search and geo-queries are essential for protection monitoring workflows; MSK managed Kafka reduces operational overhead

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS managed node groups in eu-west-1; Amazon MSK for Kafka; Amazon OpenSearch Service for Elasticsearch-compatible index
hosting_target.rationale: EKS provides managed Kubernetes for Faust consumer pods; MSK and OpenSearch reduce operational surface area

privacy_retention_policy.value: Incident reports retained in hot index 2 years, then archived to S3 with Apache Parquet; personally identifiable witness data purged from search index after 5 years per organizational policy
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Witness protection obligations require time-limited retention of identifying information in searchable systems

future_scope.value: NLP-based automatic incident classification, cross-source entity deduplication for perpetrator profiles, integration with UN OCHA ReliefWeb for public incident summaries
future_scope.rationale: ML enrichment and cross-system integration planned for Phase 2 pending pipeline stability

constraints.value: Sub-10-second latency from Kafka ingest to Elasticsearch searchable; pipeline must tolerate 2-hour Kafka connectivity gaps from field sources; no PII in Kafka message bodies — pseudonymized at ingestion
constraints.rationale: Latency SLA driven by alert response time requirements; connectivity tolerance required for intermittent field source connections; PII constraint from Do No Harm principle

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Faust consumer tracing; Prometheus metrics for Kafka consumer lag, indexing throughput, and alert rule evaluation latency; Grafana dashboards; PagerDuty on critical pipeline failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; consumer lag is the primary operational signal for pipeline health

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment with blue-green consumer rollouts; Kafka consumer group versioning for safe schema migrations
execution_preference.rationale: Stream processing pipelines require zero-downtime deployment strategies to avoid message loss

compliance_context.value: Do No Harm principle prohibiting re-identification of witnesses; organizational data classification policy for incident data; GDPR for EU-funded program data; witness protection protocols mandating data minimization in searchable systems
compliance_context.rationale: Incident data exposure could endanger witnesses and informants; legal and ethical obligations require data minimization and access restriction

project.goal: Near-real-time incident report pipeline from field ingestion to Elasticsearch index with alert rule evaluation
project.target_users: Protection monitoring analysts and field coordinators
project.timeline: MVP in 3 months, full feature set in 5 months

backend.framework: Faust 1.10 stream processing with Kafka consumer agents
backend.api: No external API; internal Kafka topics as interface contract

security.auth: AWS IAM roles per service with least-privilege policies
security.encryption: MSK TLS, EBS KMS encryption, OpenSearch encryption at rest

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache for alert rule cache and record deduplication bloom filter
data.stream: Amazon MSK (3-broker Kafka cluster, 32 partitions for incident-reports topic)

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline correctly prioritizes the sub-10-second latency SLA as the primary design constraint. Alert rule evaluation near-real-time is the key operational value for protection monitoring teams who currently wait 24 hours for daily digests. The batch backfill capability is essential for retroactively applying new alert rules to historical incident data.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Faust on EKS is a solid choice for the Python-native stream processing requirement. The main architectural risk from Round 1 was the alert rule evaluation happening inside the normalization consumer, which couples two concerns and makes rule updates require consumer redeployment. Round 2 must separate the alert evaluation into a dedicated downstream Faust agent consuming from an enriched-incidents topic.
reasoner_reviews.security: The pseudonymization at ingestion boundary is the critical security control — witness names and identifiers must be replaced with deterministic pseudonymous tokens before any message is published to the Kafka bus. The pseudonym mapping table must live outside the pipeline in a separately access-controlled store. Round 1 left this unspecified; it must be addressed in Round 2.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2-hour connectivity gap tolerance from field sources is handled at the MSK level via topic retention configured to 48 hours, but the Faust consumer offset commit strategy needs to handle reconnection without reprocessing. The DLQ must route to a separate encrypted S3 prefix, not back to the main Kafka topic.

backend.service_design: Three Faust agents deployed as separate EKS Deployments: IngestionNormalizer (consumes raw-incidents topic, applies schema validation and pseudonymization, publishes to normalized-incidents), GeospatialEnricher (consumes normalized-incidents, resolves location codes to lat/lon via GeoNames lookup cached in Redis, publishes to enriched-incidents), AlertEvaluator (consumes enriched-incidents, evaluates configured rules against sliding window state in Redis, dispatches alert payloads to alert-notifications topic)
backend.api_patterns: Kafka topic contracts as the API surface: raw-incidents (JSON, field-sourced), normalized-incidents (JSON, pseudonymized with LOINC-style incident_type codes), enriched-incidents (JSON, includes geo_centroid and admin_boundary fields), alert-notifications (JSON, dispatched to SNS for email/webhook delivery). Schema Registry enforces Avro schemas per topic.
backend.background_jobs: Weekly batch reprocessor: Airflow DAG reads raw incident records from S3 archive, re-runs normalization and enrichment with latest lookup tables, and bulk-indexes to a reprocessing alias in OpenSearch for backfill validation before alias promotion
backend.failure_handling: Each Faust agent publishes failed messages to a dedicated DLQ topic (raw-incidents-dlq, normalized-incidents-dlq); DLQ topic partitions replicated to encrypted S3 via Kafka Connect S3 Sink; dead-letter count monitored via Prometheus, PagerDuty alert on DLQ growth >50 messages/hour

security.auth_design: AWS IAM roles per EKS service account via IRSA; IngestionNormalizer role has MSK consume on raw-incidents and produce on normalized-incidents only; no cross-agent overprivileged roles
security.authorization_model: Amazon OpenSearch index-level access control: protection_analyst role has read-only on incidents-* indices; pipeline_indexer role has write-only on incidents-current index; admin role via SAML from organizational IdP through Kibana
security.privacy_controls: Pseudonymization service running as a sidecar to IngestionNormalizer: replaces witness_name, reporter_name, and contact_fields with deterministic HMAC-SHA256 tokens keyed by a secret stored in AWS Secrets Manager; original mapping table stored in isolated RDS instance with separate IAM access policy
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail for all AWS API calls; Faust agent structured logs include correlation_id, topic, partition, offset for every processed message; OpenSearch audit plugin logs all index queries with user identity

data.entities: incident_reports (id UUID, source_system, raw_payload_s3_key, received_at, status ENUM ingested/normalized/enriched/indexed/failed), normalized_incidents (incident_id, incident_type_code, perpetrator_category ENUM state_actor/armed_group/unknown, affected_population_count INT, location_code, pseudonymized_reporter_token, event_date, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, normalized_at), enriched_incidents (incident_id, geo_centroid GEOPOINT, admin_boundary_l1, admin_boundary_l2, country_iso, enriched_at), alert_rules (id UUID, rule_name, condition_json, window_minutes INT, threshold_count INT, geographic_scope, notification_channels_json, active BOOL), alert_events (id UUID, rule_id, triggered_at, matching_incident_ids_json, notification_dispatched_at)
data.storage_design: Amazon OpenSearch Service 3-node cluster (r6g.large) with incidents-current index and incidents-archive-YYYY indices; index template enforces geo_point mapping for geo_centroid; Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode for alert rule state and deduplication bloom filter; S3 Glacier for raw incident archive after 2 years
data.consistency_notes: Exactly-once semantics via Kafka transactions between IngestionNormalizer and normalized-incidents topic; AlertEvaluator uses at-least-once with idempotent alert_events insertion keyed by rule_id + window_start_epoch

devops.deployment_topology: Three Faust agent Deployments on EKS (eu-west-1): each in dedicated namespace with NetworkPolicy restricting egress to MSK and OpenSearch VPC endpoints only; HPA on Kafka consumer lag metric via KEDA
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with Faust test agent harness, kafka-python integration tests against localstack MSK emulator, Docker build and ECR push, Helm chart upgrade with blue-green consumer group rollout (deploy new consumer group, validate lag drains to zero on new group, decommission old group)
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM agent sidecar on all pods; Prometheus MSK consumer-lag metrics via kafka_exporter sidecar; Grafana dashboards: ingestion rate by source, normalization error rate, enrichment cache hit rate, alert evaluation latency p99, DLQ depth per topic
devops.cost_controls: KEDA scales consumer pods to zero outside business hours for non-realtime topics; OpenSearch UltraWarm tier for incidents older than 30 days; MSK topic retention tuned to 48 hours to minimize broker storage

ISS-001.title: Alert evaluation coupled to normalization consumer
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan placed alert rule evaluation logic inside the IngestionNormalizer Faust agent. This couples two distinct processing concerns, requires consumer redeployment for every rule change, and prevents independent scaling of the alert evaluation workload.

history.note: Identified in architect_reasoner review

ISS-002.title: Pseudonymization boundary not specified
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan stated witnesses would be pseudonymized but did not specify where in the pipeline the transformation occurs, what replaces the PII, or where the mapping table lives. This is a critical Do No Harm compliance gap.

history.note: Identified in security review

ISS-003.title: DLQ routing back to main topic creates reprocessing loop
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 DLQ design routed failed messages back to the head of the main ingestion topic, creating potential infinite reprocessing loops for structurally invalid messages.

history.note: Identified in constraints review

focus_issues.title: Pseudonymization boundary not specified
focus_issues.detail: PII pseudonymization location, transformation method, and mapping table storage are unspecified — critical Do No Harm compliance gap.

focus_issues.title: Alert evaluation coupled to normalization consumer
focus_issues.detail: Alert logic inside IngestionNormalizer prevents independent scaling and requires redeployment for rule changes.

latest_recommendations: Separate AlertEvaluator into a dedicated Faust agent consuming from a downstream enriched-incidents topic
Specify pseudonymization as a sidecar service in IngestionNormalizer with HMAC-SHA256 tokenization and isolated mapping table in RDS
Route DLQ to separate S3-backed topics, not back to the main ingestion topic

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues including one critical blocking issue

previous_audits.summary: Pipeline architecture is fundamentally sound with correct Kafka/Faust/OpenSearch technology choices, but two significant issues prevent approval: the pseudonymization boundary is completely unspecified (critical Do No Harm gap), and the alert evaluation is incorrectly coupled to the normalization consumer.
strengths: Correct use of Kafka topic partitioning for parallelism
OpenSearch geo_point mapping choice is appropriate for geographic incident clustering
MSK managed service reduces operational overhead
concerns: No specification of where or how PII pseudonymization occurs before Kafka publish
Alert rule evaluation inside normalization consumer creates coupling and scaling limitation
blocking_issues: ISS-002: pseudonymization boundary unspecified — cannot approve plan with PII potentially flowing through Kafka bus unprotected
recommendations: Add pseudonymization sidecar to IngestionNormalizer: HMAC-SHA256 tokenization of witness/reporter PII fields, mapping table in isolated RDS, secret key in Secrets Manager
Create dedicated AlertEvaluator Faust agent consuming enriched-incidents topic with Redis sliding window state
