previous_plan.title: Humanitarian Incident Report Pipeline v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Kafka-based pipeline using Faust stream processing on EKS to ingest incident reports from multiple field sources, normalize and enrich with geospatial metadata, and index to OpenSearch for near-real-time search and alerting by protection monitoring analysts.
previous_plan.system_components: Amazon MSK Kafka cluster, Faust IngestionNormalizer consumer (normalization + alert evaluation combined), GeospatialEnricher consumer, OpenSearch Service cluster, Redis ElastiCache for deduplication, S3 for raw archive.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: IAM roles for EKS service accounts, TLS between pipeline components, OpenSearch index access control. Witness data will be pseudonymized before storage. (Pseudonymization mechanism not specified in v1.)
previous_plan.workflows: Incident report submitted to raw-incidents Kafka topic, consumed by IngestionNormalizer which normalizes, evaluates alert rules, and publishes to enriched-incidents. GeospatialEnricher consumes enriched-incidents and adds geo coordinates before OpenSearch bulk index.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must address two critical issues from the audit: (1) the pseudonymization boundary must be fully specified as a sidecar service in the IngestionNormalizer with HMAC-SHA256 tokenization and an isolated mapping table, ensuring PII never enters the Kafka bus; (2) the AlertEvaluator must be extracted as a separate Faust agent consuming from the downstream enriched-incidents topic, enabling independent scaling and rule updates without consumer redeployment. The DLQ routing loop fix is also incorporated. The three-agent topology (IngestionNormalizer, GeospatialEnricher, AlertEvaluator) is the core architectural change.

fix_report.action_taken: Added PseudonymizationSidecar specification to IngestionNormalizer deployment: HMAC-SHA256 tokenization of witness_name, reporter_name, and contact_fields before Kafka publish; mapping table in isolated RDS PostgreSQL with separate IRSA policy; HMAC key stored in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. Documented in security_and_compliance and system_components sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No PII will flow through the Kafka bus; Do No Harm compliance gap resolved; pseudonymization is auditable via Secrets Manager key usage logs

fix_report.action_taken: Extracted AlertEvaluator as a dedicated third Faust agent consuming from enriched-incidents topic with Redis sliding window state. IngestionNormalizer now only handles pseudonymization and normalization. AlertEvaluator deploys independently and scales via KEDA on its own consumer lag metric. Alert rule updates require only AlertEvaluator redeployment.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Alert evaluation decoupled from normalization; independent scaling; alert rule changes deployed without touching normalization consumer

target_output.title: Humanitarian Incident Report Pipeline v2
target_output.executive_summary: A three-stage Kafka stream processing pipeline running on Amazon EKS with Faust Python agents: IngestionNormalizer (pseudonymizes PII and normalizes incident schema), GeospatialEnricher (resolves location codes to geo coordinates via Redis-cached GeoNames lookup), and AlertEvaluator (evaluates configurable alert rules against sliding window incident counts with Redis state). Incident reports flow from Amazon MSK to Amazon OpenSearch Service with sub-10-second end-to-end latency. Witness and reporter PII is tokenized at the ingestion boundary via HMAC-SHA256 before any data enters the Kafka bus, with mapping tables stored in an isolated RDS instance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three decoupled Faust stream processing agents deployed as independent EKS Deployments in eu-west-1, each consuming from and publishing to dedicated Amazon MSK Kafka topics. IngestionNormalizer consumes from raw-incidents, runs a PseudonymizationSidecar process to replace PII fields with HMAC-SHA256 tokens before publishing normalized-incidents (PII never transits Kafka). GeospatialEnricher consumes normalized-incidents, resolves location_code to geo_centroid and admin_boundary fields via Redis-cached GeoNames API, and publishes enriched-incidents. AlertEvaluator consumes enriched-incidents, maintains sliding window incident counts per geographic scope and incident type in Redis, evaluates active alert rules, and publishes triggered alerts to alert-notifications topic for SNS dispatch. All agents scale independently via KEDA on their respective Kafka consumer lag metrics. Amazon OpenSearch Service (3-node r6g.large cluster) is the final index destination, written by a dedicated OpenSearchIndexer consumer. Failed messages route to per-topic DLQ topics backed by Kafka Connect S3 Sink to encrypted S3 prefixes — never back to the main topic.
target_output.technology_stack: Stream processing: Python 3.12, Faust 1.10 (Kafka consumer agent framework). Message bus: Amazon MSK (3-broker, 32 partitions on incident-reports topic), MSK Schema Registry (Avro schemas). Index: Amazon OpenSearch Service 8-compatible (3-node r6g.large). Cache/state: Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode. Pseudonymization: HMAC-SHA256 via Python hmac module, key in AWS Secrets Manager. Mapping store: RDS PostgreSQL 16 (isolated subnet). Container platform: EKS managed node groups, KEDA for consumer-lag-based autoscaling. Observability: Datadog APM, Prometheus kafka_exporter, Grafana. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Helm 3, ECR.
target_output.functional_feature_map: PseudonymizationSidecar: replaces witness_name, reporter_name, and contact_fields with deterministic HMAC-SHA256 tokens keyed by Secrets Manager secret; mapping table in isolated RDS for authorized de-pseudonymization. IngestionNormalizer: validates raw incident JSON against Avro schema, maps source-specific field names to canonical incident_type_code and perpetrator_category enums, computes severity score, publishes to normalized-incidents. GeospatialEnricher: resolves location_code via GeoNames API with 24-hour Redis TTL cache, adds geo_centroid GEOPOINT and admin_boundary_l1/l2 fields, publishes to enriched-incidents. AlertEvaluator: maintains per-rule sliding window counters in Redis; evaluates active alert_rules against enriched incident stream; dispatches alert_events to SNS for email and webhook notification. OpenSearchIndexer: bulk-indexes enriched incidents to incidents-current with 5-second flush interval. BatchReprocessor: Airflow DAG for retroactive enrichment and reindexing of historical incidents when alert rules are updated.
target_output.system_components: Amazon MSK 3-broker Kafka cluster (raw-incidents, normalized-incidents, enriched-incidents, alert-notifications topics, plus DLQ variants), MSK Schema Registry (Avro). EKS cluster: IngestionNormalizer Deployment (Faust agent + PseudonymizationSidecar), GeospatialEnricher Deployment (Faust agent + Redis lookup), AlertEvaluator Deployment (Faust agent with Redis sliding window state), OpenSearchIndexer Deployment (bulk indexing consumer), KEDA ScaledObjects per Deployment. Amazon OpenSearch Service 3-node cluster (incidents-current + incidents-archive-YYYY indices). Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster mode (enrichment cache + alert window state + deduplication bloom filter). RDS PostgreSQL 16 (pseudonym mapping table, isolated subnet). Kafka Connect S3 Sink (DLQ export to S3). S3 encrypted buckets (raw archive, DLQ archive). Airflow on EKS (batch reprocessor DAGs). Datadog APM + Prometheus + Grafana.
target_output.workflows: Incident Ingestion and Pseudonymization: Field reporting system publishes raw JSON incident report to MSK raw-incidents topic. IngestionNormalizer Faust agent reads the message, passes PII fields to PseudonymizationSidecar which computes HMAC-SHA256 tokens and writes the original:token mapping to isolated RDS. Normalized message (all PII replaced with tokens) is validated against Avro schema and published to normalized-incidents. No PII ever appears in the Kafka bus beyond the raw-incidents topic which is restricted to ingestion IAM role read access only. Alert Rule Evaluation: AlertEvaluator consumes from enriched-incidents, increments sliding window Redis counters keyed by rule_id:geographic_scope:incident_type with configurable TTL equal to the rule window_minutes. On each message, evaluates all active alert rules from Redis cache: if counter >= threshold_count for a rule, publishes alert_event to alert-notifications topic. SNS fan-out delivers email to subscribed analysts and webhook to protection coordination platform. Rule updates by analysts via admin UI update the alert_rules table and invalidate the Redis rule cache, triggering AlertEvaluator to reload rules on next message — no redeployment required.
target_output.data_model: incident_reports (id UUID, source_system TEXT, raw_payload_s3_key TEXT, received_at DATETIME, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('ingested','normalized','enriched','indexed','failed'))). normalized_incidents (incident_id FK, incident_type_code TEXT, perpetrator_category TEXT CHECK(category IN ('state_actor','armed_group','unknown')), affected_population_count INT, location_code TEXT, pseudonymized_reporter_token TEXT, event_date DATE, severity TEXT CHECK(severity IN ('critical','high','medium','low')), normalized_at DATETIME). enriched_incidents (incident_id FK, geo_centroid GEOPOINT, admin_boundary_l1 TEXT, admin_boundary_l2 TEXT, country_iso CHAR(2), enriched_at DATETIME). alert_rules (id UUID, rule_name TEXT, condition_json TEXT, window_minutes INT, threshold_count INT, geographic_scope TEXT, notification_channels_json TEXT, active BOOL, updated_at DATETIME). alert_events (id UUID, rule_id FK, triggered_at DATETIME, matching_incident_ids_json TEXT, notification_dispatched_at DATETIME). pseudonym_mappings (token TEXT PK, original_value_encrypted TEXT, field_type TEXT, created_at DATETIME) -- stored in isolated RDS PostgreSQL, not in pipeline data store.
target_output.api_design: Kafka topic contracts (schema-registry-enforced Avro): POST to raw-incidents topic (source field systems authenticate with MSK SASL/SCRAM). Internal topic contracts: normalized-incidents schema v1 (incident_id, incident_type_code, perpetrator_category, pseudonymized_reporter_token, location_code, event_date, severity). enriched-incidents schema v1 (all normalized fields + geo_centroid, admin_boundary_l1, admin_boundary_l2). alert-notifications topic (rule_id, rule_name, triggered_at, matching_incident_count, geographic_scope). Admin REST API for alert rule management: GET /alert-rules, POST /alert-rules, PUT /alert-rules/{id}, DELETE /alert-rules/{id}. OpenSearch Query API for analysts via Kibana: GET /incidents-current/_search with geo_distance and date_range filters.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Do No Harm compliance: PseudonymizationSidecar tokenizes witness_name, reporter_name, contact_fields with HMAC-SHA256 before normalized-incidents publish; HMAC key in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation; mapping table in isolated RDS accessible only to authorized de-pseudonymization role via separate IRSA policy. Encryption at rest: MSK EBS volumes encrypted with KMS; OpenSearch Service encryption at rest enabled; RDS AES-256; S3 SSE-KMS for raw archive and DLQ buckets. Encryption in transit: MSK TLS 1.3; OpenSearch HTTPS; Redis AUTH + TLS; RDS SSL. IAM least-privilege: per-agent IRSA roles with MSK produce/consume scoped to specific topics; OpenSearch fine-grained access control with protection_analyst (read) and pipeline_indexer (write) roles; SAML SSO for Kibana analyst access via organizational IdP. Audit: CloudTrail for all AWS API calls; Faust agent structured logs with correlation_id per message; OpenSearch audit plugin logs all index queries.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster in eu-west-1 with 3 node groups: general workloads (m6i.xlarge), memory-optimized for AlertEvaluator (r6i.xlarge). Each Faust agent deployed as a Helm-managed Kubernetes Deployment in dedicated namespace. NetworkPolicy restricts pod egress to MSK VPC endpoint, OpenSearch VPC endpoint, Redis endpoint, and Secrets Manager endpoint only. KEDA ScaledObjects autoscale each Deployment on Kafka consumer lag: min 2 replicas, max 10. GitHub Actions CI: pytest with Faust test agent harness + kafka-python integration tests against LocalStack MSK emulator; Docker build and ECR push; Helm upgrade with blue-green consumer group rollout strategy. Blue-green rollout: deploy new consumer group version, monitor via Grafana that new group's lag drains to zero while old group is decommissioned. Schema migrations: Schema Registry compatibility mode set to BACKWARD; new Avro schema versions deployed and validated before consumer rollout.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: distributed traces from MSK message receipt through normalization, enrichment, and indexing with trace_id propagated in Kafka message headers. Prometheus metrics via kafka_exporter sidecar: kafka_consumer_lag_sum per consumer group and topic (primary health signal), incidents_normalized_total, incidents_enriched_total, enrichment_cache_hit_ratio, alert_evaluations_total, alert_events_triggered_total, dlq_messages_total per topic. Grafana dashboards: pipeline throughput by stage, consumer lag waterfall, alert rule firing rate by geographic scope, DLQ depth heatmap. PagerDuty alerts: consumer lag > 500 messages for >5 minutes (pipeline stall), DLQ growth > 50/hour (systematic ingestion errors), OpenSearch indexing error rate > 1% (index health), AlertEvaluator pod count < 1 (alert evaluation down).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MSK 3-broker kafka.m5.large: ~$450/month; OpenSearch 3x r6g.large: ~$600/month; EKS nodes at steady state 6x m6i.xlarge: ~$900/month; KEDA scales to zero for non-realtime batch consumers outside business hours saving ~30% EKS cost. OpenSearch UltraWarm tier for incidents older than 30 days reduces hot node storage cost by 80%. Total estimated ~$2,200/month for 5,000 incident reports/day. At 50,000/day: MSK partition increase (no cost change), OpenSearch add 2 data nodes (~$400), EKS KEDA scales consumers (+$300/month dynamic).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (already deployed, v1): Single-source Kafka ingestion, basic normalization, OpenSearch indexing, combined normalization+alert consumer. Phase 2 MVP fix (this round, 4 weeks): Pseudonymization sidecar with RDS mapping table, AlertEvaluator separation as dedicated Faust agent, DLQ routing to S3-backed topics, KEDA autoscaling for all agents. Phase 3 (6 weeks): GeospatialEnricher with GeoNames API and Redis TTL cache, multi-source Kafka ingestion (3 additional field reporting systems onboarded), Avro schema registry enforcement, batch reprocessor Airflow DAG. Phase 4 (8 weeks): NLP-based incident classification via SageMaker endpoint integration, perpetrator profile deduplication across sources, Kibana analyst dashboard with saved alert rule templates.
target_output.development_guidelines: Faust agent tests use faust.contrib.pytest test_agent harness with mock Kafka topics; each agent must have unit tests covering normalization field mapping, pseudonymization token consistency (same input always produces same token), alert rule threshold logic, and DLQ routing on schema validation failure. Python type hints enforced via mypy in CI with strict mode. Pre-commit hooks: black, isort, flake8. Avro schema changes require Schema Registry compatibility check before merge. Blue-green consumer group rollout mandatory for all production consumer deployments. ADR required for any change to pseudonymization algorithm or key rotation strategy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HMAC-SHA256 pseudonymization is deterministic — if the HMAC key is compromised, all tokens are re-identifiable. Mitigation: 90-day key rotation with re-tokenization batch job; Secrets Manager access logged to CloudTrail; mapping table in isolated RDS with break-glass access policy. Risk: GeoNames API rate limits for GeospatialEnricher during high-volume ingestion spikes. Mitigation: Redis 24-hour TTL cache covers 95%+ of location codes in steady state; circuit breaker skips enrichment and publishes with null geo fields rather than blocking the pipeline. Tradeoff: Avro schema registry adds operational complexity but is required for backward-compatible schema evolution across independent agent deployments. Tradeoff: KEDA scale-to-zero for batch consumers saves cost but introduces cold-start latency of 30-60 seconds on first message after idle period.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose HMAC-SHA256 over format-preserving encryption for pseudonymization: determinism simplifies cross-source deduplication at the cost of re-identification risk if key is compromised — accepted with 90-day rotation mitigation. Confirmed DLQ route to S3-backed Kafka topics (not requeue to main topic) to prevent reprocessing loops. Chose KEDA over HPA for autoscaling: consumer lag is a more semantically meaningful scale signal than CPU for stream processing workloads.

metadata.notes: Round 2 incident report pipeline: resolves critical pseudonymization gap and decouples AlertEvaluator into separate Faust agent.

project_goal.value: API service aggregating and exposing harmonized financial accountability data for NGO projects — expenditures, budget variances, and audit findings — to donor platforms and oversight bodies via a standardized REST API
project_goal.rationale: Donor platforms need programmatic access to NGO financial data in a standardized format to reduce manual reporting overhead

target_users.value: Donor platform integrations, institutional oversight bodies, and authorized NGO finance teams
target_users.rationale: External consumers are API integrators, not direct end users; NGO staff use as data publishers

project_class.rationale: Primary value is API consumption by external systems; no primary user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: REST API with partner authentication, financial data aggregation, and analytics-grade query support

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-tenant financial data aggregation with IATI-compliant schema transformation and budget variance calculation logic

risk_level.rationale: Financial accountability data exposure to external partners requires strict access control and audit trail

data_sensitivity.rationale: Contains project budgets, expenditure records, audit findings, and disbursement data

external_exposure.rationale: API consumed by vetted donor platforms and oversight bodies with OAuth2 client credentials authentication

access_model.value: OAuth2 client credentials for machine-to-machine partner access; scopes: financial_reports:read, audit_findings:read, expenditures:write for NGO data submission
access_model.rationale: Partner platforms need automated API access without user delegation; scopes enforce least-privilege data access

feature_scope.value: Financial report submission by NGO partners, budget variance calculation, audit finding ingestion, expenditure line aggregation, IATI-compliant data export, paginated query API for donors, data freshness timestamps, project-level access scoping
feature_scope.rationale: Full financial accountability lifecycle from NGO report submission to donor query

mvp_scope.value: Financial report submission endpoint, budget variance computation, paginated expenditure query API with project_code scoping, OAuth2 client credentials auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum feature set to replace manual CSV email exchanges between NGOs and donor platforms

security_baseline.value: OAuth2 client credentials with Azure AD, TLS 1.3 for all API traffic, row-level security in PostgreSQL by donor_organization_id, API rate limiting, request signing for sensitive mutation endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Financial data access requires per-partner scoping and rate limiting to prevent data scraping

frontend_stack.value: None — API service with no primary UI; API documentation via Springdoc OpenAPI 3.1 UI
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service project class; consumers are external systems not browser users

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.2 and Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server
backend_stack.rationale: Spring Boot provides mature OAuth2 resource server support and Spring Data JPA for financial entity modeling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server; Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis for query result caching
data_platform.rationale: Azure-native managed PostgreSQL for financial relational data with row-level security; Redis for expensive aggregation query caching

hosting_target.value: Azure App Service (P2v3) in West Europe region with Azure Front Door for TLS termination and WAF
hosting_target.rationale: Azure App Service simplifies Java deployment without Kubernetes overhead at this scale; Front Door provides WAF for partner-facing API

privacy_retention_policy.value: Financial report data retained 7 years per EU financial regulation; audit findings retained indefinitely; personal data in signatory fields anonymized after 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: EU-funded project financial records subject to 7-year retention requirement under EU Financial Regulation

future_scope.value: Real-time webhooks for budget variance threshold breaches, ML-based anomaly detection for expenditure patterns, IATI registry direct submission integration
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements pending API stability and partner onboarding completion

constraints.value: IATI standard compliance for data export format; response time under 2 seconds for all paginated queries; support up to 200 concurrent partner API connections
constraints.rationale: IATI compliance required for EU donor integration; performance SLA driven by donor platform polling frequency

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Application Insights for API tracing; custom metrics for financial report submission rate and query latency by partner; Azure Alerts for error rate thresholds
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate Azure monitoring standard; partner-specific metrics needed for SLA reporting

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment via Azure DevOps pipelines with blue-green slot swapping on Azure App Service
execution_preference.rationale: App Service deployment slots enable zero-downtime releases for partner-facing API

compliance_context.value: IATI standard reporting requirements for donor-funded project data; EU financial regulation 2018/1046 for 7-year retention; GDPR for any personal data in financial records; donor financial accountability frameworks (USAID ADS, ECHO financial guidelines)
compliance_context.rationale: Multi-donor API must satisfy different donors' financial accountability requirements in a unified data model

project.goal: REST API exposing harmonized NGO financial accountability data to donor platforms with IATI-compliant export
project.target_users: Donor platform integrations and oversight bodies via OAuth2 client credentials

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Data JPA, Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server

security.auth: OAuth2 client credentials via Azure AD; scopes: financial_reports:read, audit_findings:read, expenditures:write
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 via Azure Front Door; PostgreSQL SSL; Redis AUTH+TLS

data.db: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server with row-level security
data.cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7 (query result caching, 5-minute TTL for paginated queries)

devops.hosting: Azure App Service P2v3 with deployment slots

reasoner_reviews.product: The IATI-compliant export is the highest-value feature for donor platform integrations. The Round 1 plan defined the data model correctly but omitted the budget variance calculation logic entirely from the plan sections despite it being in the feature scope. This must be addressed in Round 2.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Boot 3.2 with Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server is a mature choice for this use case. The primary architectural concern from Round 1 is that the Row-Level Security policy in PostgreSQL was described but the Spring Data JPA integration for multi-tenant RLS filtering was not specified — the application must set the app.current_donor_id session variable before each query. Round 2 must document this integration explicitly.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth2 scope enforcement is correctly defined. The missing element from Round 1 is request signing for the financial report submission endpoint (expenditures:write scope) — mutation of financial records requires HMAC request signatures from NGO partners to prevent replay attacks. This was listed in the security_baseline but not implemented in the api_design section.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2-second response SLA for paginated queries is achievable with Redis query caching but the cache invalidation strategy for financial reports (when an NGO submits a new report, cached aggregations must be invalidated) was not addressed in Round 1.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot 3.2 application with domain packages: financial_reports (submission, validation, status tracking), expenditures (line items, aggregation queries), budget_variance (calculation service, threshold evaluation), audit_findings (ingestion, status management), iati_export (IATI 2.03 XML transformation service)
backend.api_patterns: REST API versioned at /api/v1; Spring Data JPA repositories with custom @Query for complex financial aggregations; @Cacheable on paginated query methods with Redis TTL 5 minutes; cache eviction on financial report status change
backend.business_modules: BudgetVarianceService: computes (approved_budget - sum(expenditure_lines)) / approved_budget per project per reporting period, flags variances exceeding configurable thresholds. IATIExportService: transforms internal financial model to IATI 2.03 ActivityRecord XML using JAXB with custom namespace mapping.
backend.failure_handling: @ControllerAdvice global exception handler mapping domain exceptions to RFC 7807 Problem JSON responses; async financial report processing with Spring @Async and status polling endpoint; idempotency via client-provided idempotency_key header on write endpoints

security.auth_design: Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server validates JWT bearer tokens issued by Azure AD; token introspection endpoint called on first request per token, result cached 5 minutes in Redis
security.authorization_model: OAuth2 scopes enforced via @PreAuthorize annotations: financial_reports:read on GET endpoints, expenditures:write on POST /expenditure-reports, audit_findings:read on GET /audit-findings. PostgreSQL RLS policy: CREATE POLICY donor_isolation ON financial_reports USING (donor_organization_id = current_setting('app.current_donor_id')::UUID). Spring Data JPA EntityManager interceptor sets app.current_donor_id from OAuth token claim before each query.
security.abuse_prevention: Azure Front Door WAF with OWASP rule set; Spring rate limiter (Bucket4j) at 100 requests/minute per client_id; request body size limit 10MB for report submissions
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured JSON logging via Logback with MDC: partner_client_id, request_id, project_code, operation logged on every API call; Application Insights correlation for distributed traces; financial mutation events emitted to Azure Event Hub for compliance audit trail

data.entities: project_budgets (id UUID, project_code TEXT UNIQUE, donor_organization_id UUID FK, approved_amount DECIMAL(15,2), currency CHAR(3), approval_date DATE, status ENUM active/closed/suspended), expenditure_lines (id UUID, report_id FK, budget_line_code TEXT, amount DECIMAL(15,2), currency CHAR(3), period_start DATE, period_end DATE, description TEXT, supporting_doc_ref TEXT), financial_reports (id UUID, project_code FK, ngo_partner_id UUID, reporting_period_start DATE, reporting_period_end DATE, total_expenditure DECIMAL(15,2), currency CHAR(3), status ENUM submitted/under_review/accepted/rejected, submitted_at DATETIME), audit_findings (id UUID, project_code FK, finding_type ENUM compliance/financial/operational, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, description TEXT, remediation_deadline DATE, status ENUM open/remediated/accepted, issued_at DATETIME), budget_revisions (id UUID, project_code FK, previous_amount DECIMAL(15,2), revised_amount DECIMAL(15,2), revision_reason TEXT, approved_by TEXT, revision_date DATE)
data.storage_design: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (General Purpose 4 vCores, 16 GB) with row-level security policies per donor_organization_id; Azure Cache for Redis 7 (C2 Standard) for query result caching with 5-minute TTL; Azure Blob Storage for financial report document attachments
data.analytics_events: BudgetVarianceExceeded event emitted when variance > 15%; AuditFindingEscalated event when critical finding approaches remediation deadline; monthly expenditure aggregate materialized view refreshed nightly

devops.deployment_topology: Azure App Service P2v3 in West Europe with staging and production deployment slots; Azure Front Door Premium with WAF policy in front of both slots; Azure Database Flexible Server with high-availability zone-redundant standby
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: Maven build, JUnit + Spring Boot Test integration tests with Testcontainers PostgreSQL, Docker image to Azure Container Registry, deploy to staging slot, smoke tests, swap production slot. Blue-green via slot swap with pre-swap health check validation.

previous_plan.title: NGO Financial Accountability API v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Spring Boot 3.2 REST API on Azure App Service exposing NGO financial accountability data to donor platforms via OAuth2 client credentials authentication. Covers report submission, expenditure queries, and IATI-compliant export.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 client credentials with Azure AD. PostgreSQL row-level security policy filtering by donor_organization_id. TLS 1.3 via Azure Front Door. Budget variance and audit findings restricted to authorized donor scopes.
previous_plan.api_design: GET /api/v1/projects/{code}/financial-reports, POST /api/v1/expenditure-reports, GET /api/v1/expenditure-reports?project_code={code}&period_start={date}, GET /api/v1/audit-findings?project_code={code}, GET /api/v1/iati-export?project_code={code}. OAuth2 bearer token required. Rate limit 100 req/min per client_id.
previous_plan.data_model: project_budgets, expenditure_lines, financial_reports, audit_findings, budget_revisions tables with donor_organization_id foreign key for RLS scoping.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a flawed plan — I need to address the two high-severity issues while introducing a subtle remaining flaw consistent with the manifest's plan_quality of 'flawed'. I will correctly specify BudgetVarianceService and the JPA/RLS integration (fixing ISS-001 and ISS-002), but the IATI export section will incorrectly reference IATI 2.03 XML while the data model uses a schema that is actually incompatible with IATI's iati-activity element structure (ISS-003 request signing will remain partially unaddressed — the signing header is mentioned but validation logic is not specified in the backend code).

Added BudgetVarianceScheduledJob for nightly pre-computation to meet 2-second response SLA.

Documented in security_and_compliance and architecture_overview.

target_output.title: NGO Financial Accountability API v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Spring Boot 3.2 REST API on Azure App Service exposing harmonized NGO financial accountability data to donor platforms via OAuth2 client credentials authentication with Azure AD. Covers financial report submission by NGO partners, budget variance computation via BudgetVarianceService, expenditure line querying, audit finding management, and IATI 2.03-compliant data export. Multi-tenant isolation enforced via PostgreSQL row-level security with donor_organization_id session variable injected by a Spring HandlerInterceptor from validated JWT claims.
target_output.architecture_overview: Spring Boot 3.2 application deployed on Azure App Service P2v3 behind Azure Front Door Premium (TLS termination, WAF, rate limiting). Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server validates JWT bearer tokens from Azure AD tenant. DonorTenantInterceptor extracts donor_organization_id from the validated JWT and executes SET LOCAL app.current_donor_id = :donorId via a native EntityManager query before each JPA operation, activating the PostgreSQL RLS policy for all subsequent queries in that request transaction. Domain service layer: BudgetVarianceService computes (approved_budget_amount - sum(expenditure_lines.amount)) / approved_budget_amount * 100 per project per period with nightly pre-computation; IATIExportService transforms financial records to IATI 2.03 XML using JAXB. Redis 7 via Azure Cache caches paginated query results for 5 minutes with @CacheEvict triggered on financial report status transitions.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Java 21 on Azure App Service P2v3. Framework: Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Data JPA 3.2, Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server, Springdoc OpenAPI 3.1. Database: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server with RLS. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7 via Lettuce client. Auth: Azure AD OAuth2 with client credentials flow. Rate limiting: Bucket4j 8.x via Spring AOP. JAXB for IATI XML. Build: Maven 3.9, Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: Micrometer + Application Insights Java agent.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Financial Report Submission: NGO partner POSTs quarterly expenditure report with HMAC-SHA256 signature header; service validates signature, persists report, triggers BudgetVarianceService recalculation, evicts Redis cache for affected project_code. Budget Variance API: GET /api/v1/projects/{code}/budget-variance returns variance_pct, variance_amount_usd, threshold_status ENUM (within_budget/minor_variance/material_variance/critical_variance); thresholds configurable per donor_agreement (default 10%/15%/25%). Expenditure Query: paginated GET with project_code, reporting_period, budget_line_code filters; results cached in Redis for 5 minutes per (donor_id, project_code, period) cache key. Audit Finding Management: ingestion of audit findings by authorized oversight bodies, status tracking through remediation lifecycle. IATI Export: GET /api/v1/iati-export?project_code={code} returns IATI 2.03 XML ActivityRecord with transaction elements mapped from expenditure_lines. Note: the IATI export mapping uses budget_line_code as the transaction description, which is not compliant with IATI transaction-type vocabulary — this is a known mapping gap flagged for Phase 2 remediation.
target_output.system_components: Azure Front Door Premium (WAF, TLS termination, rate limiting), Azure App Service P2v3 (Spring Boot application, production + staging slots), DonorTenantInterceptor (Spring HandlerInterceptor setting PostgreSQL session variable per request), BudgetVarianceService (computation + nightly pre-computation via @Scheduled), IATIExportService (JAXB XML transformation), Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (RLS-enabled, zone-redundant HA standby), Azure Cache for Redis 7 (query result cache + OAuth token introspection cache), Azure Blob Storage (financial report document attachments), Application Insights Java agent (distributed tracing), Azure Event Hub (compliance audit trail for financial mutations), BudgetVarianceScheduledJob (nightly materialized view refresh via Quartz).
target_output.workflows: Financial Report Submission: NGO partner sends POST /api/v1/expenditure-reports with Authorization: Bearer {token} and X-HMAC-Signature: {hmac_sha256_of_body}. Spring Security validates JWT, DonorTenantInterceptor sets session variable, @PreAuthorize checks expenditures:write scope. Service validates HMAC signature using partner's registered signing key (stored in Azure Key Vault). Report persisted, BudgetVarianceService.recalculate(project_code) invoked asynchronously, Redis cache evicted via @CacheEvict(key='#projectCode'). Donor Query Flow: Donor platform sends GET /api/v1/projects/{code}/budget-variance with client credentials token. DonorTenantInterceptor sets donor_organization_id in session, RLS policy filters project_budgets to rows where donor_organization_id matches. BudgetVarianceService returns pre-computed variance from materialized view if last_computed_at within 1 hour, else computes on demand. Result cached in Redis for 5 minutes.
target_output.data_model: project_budgets (id UUID PK, project_code TEXT UNIQUE, donor_organization_id UUID FK donor_organizations, approved_amount DECIMAL(15,2), currency CHAR(3), approval_date DATE, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('active','closed','suspended'))). expenditure_lines (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK financial_reports, budget_line_code TEXT, amount DECIMAL(15,2), currency CHAR(3), period_start DATE, period_end DATE, description TEXT, supporting_doc_ref TEXT). financial_reports (id UUID PK, project_code TEXT FK, ngo_partner_id UUID FK ngo_partners, reporting_period_start DATE, reporting_period_end DATE, total_expenditure DECIMAL(15,2), currency CHAR(3), status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('submitted','under_review','accepted','rejected')), idempotency_key TEXT UNIQUE, submitted_at DATETIME). audit_findings (id UUID PK, project_code TEXT FK, finding_type TEXT CHECK(type IN ('compliance','financial','operational')), severity TEXT CHECK(severity IN ('critical','high','medium','low')), description TEXT, remediation_deadline DATE, status TEXT CHECK(status IN ('open','remediated','accepted')), issued_at DATETIME). budget_revisions (id UUID PK, project_code TEXT FK, previous_amount DECIMAL(15,2), revised_amount DECIMAL(15,2), revision_reason TEXT, approved_by TEXT, revision_date DATE). budget_variance_cache (project_code TEXT PK, donor_organization_id UUID, variance_pct DECIMAL(6,2), variance_amount DECIMAL(15,2), last_computed_at DATETIME).
target_output.api_design: Base path /api/v1. POST /expenditure-reports (scope: expenditures:write, header: X-HMAC-Signature required, body: {project_code, reporting_period_start, reporting_period_end, expenditure_lines[], idempotency_key}) -> 202 Accepted with report_id and status_url. GET /projects/{code}/financial-reports?status={status}&page={n}&size={20} (scope: financial_reports:read) -> paginated FinancialReportSummary[]. GET /projects/{code}/budget-variance (scope: financial_reports:read) -> {project_code, approved_amount, cumulative_expenditure, variance_pct, threshold_status, last_computed_at}. GET /expenditure-reports/{id}/lines?page={n} (scope: financial_reports:read) -> paginated ExpenditureLine[]. GET /audit-findings?project_code={code}&severity={severity} (scope: audit_findings:read) -> paginated AuditFinding[]. POST /audit-findings (scope: audit_findings:write) -> 201 Created. GET /iati-export?project_code={code} (scope: financial_reports:read) -> IATI 2.03 XML. Note: X-HMAC-Signature header required for POST /expenditure-reports but server-side validation logic for the signature is not yet documented in this plan.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth2 client credentials: Azure AD issues JWT access tokens to registered partner applications; Spring Security Resource Server validates token signature against Azure AD JWKS endpoint, caches result 5 minutes in Redis. DonorTenantInterceptor: implements Spring HandlerInterceptor.preHandle(), extracts donor_organization_id claim from SecurityContext, executes entityManager.createNativeQuery('SET LOCAL app.current_donor_id = :id').setParameter('id', donorId).executeUpdate() within request transaction. PostgreSQL RLS policy activates on all SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE against financial tables filtering by current_setting('app.current_donor_id')::UUID. Scope enforcement via @PreAuthorize('hasAuthority(SCOPE_financial_reports:read)') on GET endpoints. Request signing: POST endpoints with expenditures:write scope require X-HMAC-Signature header (HMAC-SHA256 of request body using partner's key from Azure Key Vault). IATI compliance: IATI 2.03 XML export with budget_line_code mapped to transaction description — note this mapping does not use the IATI transaction-type controlled vocabulary, which is a partial compliance gap. EU financial regulation: 7-year retention enforced via Azure Backup policy on PostgreSQL Flexible Server. GDPR: signatory name fields anonymized after 5 years via scheduled job.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure App Service P2v3 in West Europe with production and staging deployment slots. Azure Front Door Premium in front of both slots with WAF policy (OWASP 3.2 rule set) and custom rate limit rule: 100 req/min per X-Client-Id header. Azure DevOps Pipelines: Maven build, Testcontainers-based integration tests (PostgreSQL 16 container with RLS policies applied), Docker image to Azure Container Registry, deploy to staging slot, run smoke test suite (auth, budget variance endpoint, IATI export), perform slot swap to production. Blue-green deployment via App Service slot swap: zero-downtime for partner integrations. Azure Database Flexible Server: zone-redundant HA standby in secondary availability zone, automated backup with 35-day retention, point-in-time restore. Secrets in Azure Key Vault: partner HMAC signing keys, database credentials, Redis AUTH token. Schema migrations via Flyway on application startup (validate-before-migrate mode).
target_output.observability: Application Insights Java agent: distributed traces from HTTP request through JPA queries with correlation_id propagated. Micrometer custom metrics: financial_report_submissions_total (labeled by partner_id, status), budget_variance_calculations_total (labeled by project_code), iati_export_requests_total, api_response_time_p99 by endpoint. Azure Monitor Alerts: error_rate > 2% on any endpoint (PagerDuty), p99_latency > 3 seconds (Slack warning), budget_variance_job_failure (PagerDuty critical). Application Insights workbook with per-partner API usage, submission success rate, and variance calculation failure breakdown.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Azure App Service P2v3 (2 vCores, 8 GB): ~$140/month. Azure Database PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 4 vCores: ~$380/month. Azure Cache for Redis C2 Standard: ~$100/month. Azure Front Door Premium: ~$200/month base plus per-request fees. Total ~$820/month at steady state for 200 concurrent partners. Scale path: App Service auto-scale to P3v3 on CPU > 70%; PostgreSQL read replica for analytics queries under load.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (delivered, v1): Core financial report submission, expenditure query API, OAuth2 auth, PostgreSQL RLS, basic IATI export. Phase 2 (this round, 6 weeks): Budget variance service with nightly pre-computation, DonorTenantInterceptor for RLS/JPA integration, Redis cache with eviction strategy, HMAC request signing enforcement, audit finding API. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Real-time webhooks for variance threshold breaches via Azure Event Grid, IATI transaction-type vocabulary compliance remediation, NGO partner self-service API key management portal.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 records for DTO types; no Lombok in new code. @Transactional boundaries documented in service Javadoc — DonorTenantInterceptor must run within the same transaction as JPA queries (use REQUIRES transaction propagation). Testcontainers PostgreSQL with RLS policies applied in test schema migration for integration tests. Mockito for unit tests of BudgetVarianceService formula logic. OpenAPI 3.1 spec generated via Springdoc and committed to repo as contract. Flyway migration naming: V{version}__{description}.sql, no DDL in application code. ADR required for any change to OAuth scope definitions or RLS policy structure.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DonorTenantInterceptor fails to set session variable if the JPA operation executes outside a transaction context, causing RLS to block the query. Mitigation: @Transactional on all service methods, integration test suite validates RLS isolation per donor. Risk: Budget variance pre-computation job reads stale data if financial report submission and nightly job race. Mitigation: @CacheEvict on report status acceptance triggers immediate variance recalculation for the affected project. Tradeoff: Synchronous IATI export for large project histories may exceed the 2-second SLA for projects with >5,000 expenditure lines — async export with polling endpoint deferred to Phase 3. Known flaw: IATI transaction-type vocabulary mapping is incomplete in current implementation; budget_line_code is used as transaction description instead of IATI 2.03 transaction-type controlled vocabulary codes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Azure Database Flexible Server over Azure SQL for PostgreSQL RLS compatibility; RLS is a native PostgreSQL feature not available in Azure SQL. Confirmed Redis for both OAuth token introspection caching and query result caching to reduce infrastructure components. Chose App Service over AKS for simplified Java deployment at this concurrency scale (200 partners). IATI transaction-type vocabulary gap acknowledged and deferred to Phase 3 roadmap.

metadata.notes: Round 2 NGO financial API: fixes budget variance and RLS/JPA issues but retains IATI vocabulary mapping gap and incomplete HMAC signing validation spec as intentional flaws.

profile.domain: Legal Aid for Asylum Seekers Static Website

project_goal.value: Low-cost static website for a community-based organization providing free legal aid to asylum seekers, presenting information on available services, eligibility criteria, and how to book appointments, with content managed through a headless CMS
project_goal.rationale: Replaces an outdated WordPress site with a fast, low-maintenance static site that non-technical staff can update

target_users.value: Asylum seekers seeking legal aid services and non-technical staff managing content updates
target_users.rationale: Dual audience: public information consumers and internal content editors

project_class.rationale: Purely informational site with no server-side runtime, no auth, and no database; content served as static HTML

capabilities.rationale: Static HTML/CSS/JS with anonymous page view analytics; no backend capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with headless CMS integration; no dynamic server rendering or user accounts

risk_level.rationale: No personal data collected; public informational content only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Site collects no user data; appointment booking links to external system not managed here

external_exposure.rationale: Publicly accessible to asylum seekers without any authentication

access_model.value: Fully public, no authentication; headless CMS editor access managed separately by CMS provider
access_model.rationale: Public information site requires no user accounts

feature_scope.value: Service description pages, eligibility criteria guides, appointment booking link, news and updates section, staff profiles, multilingual content for primary spoken languages in the served community
feature_scope.rationale: Core information needs identified in community consultations

mvp_scope.value: Service pages, eligibility guides, appointment booking external link, basic CMS for staff content updates
mvp_scope.rationale: Essential content to replace old site; multilingual deferred to post-launch

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Netlify, basic security headers via netlify.toml, no third-party tracking scripts
security_baseline.rationale: Low-risk static site; standard static hosting security posture

frontend_stack.value: Hugo with Tailwind CSS
frontend_stack.rationale: Hugo generates fast static HTML from CMS content; Tailwind for maintainable styling without a build-time JS framework

backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime; Hugo generates pure HTML at build time

data_platform.value: none - headless CMS (Decap CMS) with Git-backed content
data_platform.rationale: Git-backed CMS keeps content in the repository without an external database

hosting_target.value: Netlify CDN with automatic deploys from GitHub
hosting_target.rationale: Free tier covers traffic needs; CDN delivery ensures fast load times internationally

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected or retained; anonymous Netlify Analytics page view counts only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No data governance overhead on a zero-data-collection static site

future_scope.value: Multilingual content in Somali, Arabic, and Tigrinya; appointment self-booking form with a form backend
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap based on community language needs and appointment demand

constraints.value: Must operate on free or near-free hosting; non-technical staff must be able to update content without developer assistance
constraints.rationale: Organization has no dedicated IT staff and minimal budget

observability_baseline.value: Netlify Analytics for page view counts; Netlify build notifications to Slack for deploy success or failure
observability_baseline.rationale: Minimal observability appropriate for a low-risk static site

execution_preference.value: Single delivery sprint with staff training on CMS editing before handoff
execution_preference.rationale: Simple static site allows single-sprint delivery; CMS training is the key handoff activity

project.goal: Fast informational static site for asylum seeker legal aid services
project.target_users: Asylum seekers and non-technical content editors
project.timeline: Single delivery sprint plus CMS training

frontend.approach: Static HTML generated from Markdown content files managed via Decap CMS

reasoner_reviews.product: Scope is right-sized for a community legal aid organization. The eligibility criteria pages are the highest-value content — asylum seekers need to quickly determine if they qualify before making contact. A prominent call-to-action linking to the appointment booking system on every service page will reduce time-to-contact. Staff profiles build trust with vulnerable visitors and should include photos and language skills to help asylum seekers identify a caseworker they can communicate with.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hugo with Decap CMS on Netlify is an excellent fit for this use case. Hugo builds are fast (under 5 seconds for hundreds of pages), Decap CMS provides a Git-backed editor UI that non-technical staff can use without touching code, and Netlify deploy previews allow staff to review content changes before publishing. The key configuration decision is setting up Hugo content archetypes for service_page and news_article types so staff have consistent templates.
reasoner_reviews.security: For a public informational site with no user data collection, the main security surface is content injection. The Content-Security-Policy header in netlify.toml restricting script-src to self is the key control. No external tracking scripts should be added. Appointment booking links should use rel=noopener.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Netlify free tier supports 100GB bandwidth/month which will be sufficient for expected traffic. Decap CMS is free and open source. Hugo is free. Total cost is domain registration only, typically under $15/year.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo site with layouts directory for base templates, partials for reusable components (header, footer, service-card, eligibility-checklist), and content directory with Markdown files organized by section (services/, news/, staff/). Tailwind CSS compiled via Hugo Pipes at build time.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home page with featured services and appointment CTA. /services/ section with individual pages per service area (asylum claims, detention support, family reunification). /eligibility/ guide pages. /staff/ directory. /news/ for updates. /book/ redirect page with rel=noopener to external appointment system.
frontend.accessibility_notes: Semantic HTML5 with proper heading hierarchy. All images have descriptive alt attributes. Color contrast meets WCAG AA. No JavaScript-dependent navigation.
frontend.performance_notes: Hugo static HTML with no client-side framework. Tailwind CSS purged to under 10KB. Images optimized via Hugo image processing. Netlify CDN delivers assets from edge nodes geographically close to visitors.

security.auth_design: No authentication on public site. Decap CMS editor authentication handled by Netlify Identity, separate from the public site.
security.privacy_controls: No cookies on the public site. Netlify Analytics uses server-side counting without client-side scripts or cookies. No third-party analytics or social media sharing buttons.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Netlify build logs retained for 30 days for deploy debugging. No application-level logging.

data.entities: No database. Content stored as Markdown files in the GitHub repository: service_pages (title, description, eligibility_criteria, languages_served, booking_link), news_articles (title, date, summary, body), staff_profiles (name, languages_spoken, specialization, photo). CMS config in /static/admin/config.yml defining collections for each content type.
data.retention_and_deletion: Netlify Analytics aggregated page view counts retained per Netlify policy. No personal data stored.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub repository with Hugo source and Markdown content. Netlify build: hugo --minify on push to main. Netlify CDN distributes /public output globally. Decap CMS served from /static/admin/ as a static SPA authenticated via Netlify Identity.
devops.ci_cd_design: Netlify CI triggers on every push and pull request. PRs generate deploy-preview URLs for content review. Main branch deploys to production. Build command: hugo --minify. Publish directory: public. Build time under 10 seconds.
devops.environments: Deploy previews for every pull request. Production at root domain on main branch merge.

target_output.thinking_summary: Simple Hugo static site for a legal aid organization. Key decisions: Decap CMS for Git-backed non-technical editing, Netlify free tier for zero-cost CDN hosting, Hugo content archetypes for service and news types to guide staff editors. No server runtime, no database, no user data collection. Security posture is basic hardened headers via netlify.toml. Accessibility and multilingual readability are important for the asylum seeker audience.
target_output.title: Asylum Seeker Legal Aid Static Website
target_output.executive_summary: A Hugo static site with Decap CMS hosted on Netlify, providing publicly accessible information on legal aid services, asylum claim eligibility criteria, and appointment booking for a community organization serving asylum seekers. Non-technical staff manage all content via a Git-backed CMS editor without developer involvement. Zero data collection, fast CDN delivery, and free-tier hosting aligned to the organization's budget constraints.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hugo static site generator reads Markdown content files committed to GitHub and produces HTML/CSS at build time via Netlify CI. Decap CMS provides a browser-based editor UI served from /static/admin/ authenticated via Netlify Identity, writing changes back to the GitHub repository as commits. Netlify CDN distributes the built /public directory globally. No Node.js or server process runs in production; all content is pre-rendered HTML. Tailwind CSS is compiled by Hugo Pipes during build, purged to the minimal used class set. Netlify Analytics provides server-side page view counts without client-side scripts.
target_output.technology_stack: Static site generator: Hugo 0.123 with Tailwind CSS 3.4 compiled via Hugo Pipes. CMS: Decap CMS with Netlify Identity for editor authentication. Hosting and CDN: Netlify free tier. CI: Netlify CI with deploy previews for pull requests. Repository: GitHub. Analytics: Netlify Analytics (server-side, no client script). Domain: custom domain with Netlify DNS and auto-provisioned Let's Encrypt TLS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Services Section: individual pages for asylum claim representation, immigration detention support, and family reunification cases, each with description, eligibility summary, and appointment booking CTA linking to external booking system. Eligibility Guide: structured checklist pages for each service area indicating whether a visitor may qualify based on their situation. Staff Directory: profiles listing each caseworker's name, languages spoken, and specialization. News and Updates: time-stamped articles for policy changes and office announcements. Appointment Booking Redirect: prominent CTA on every service page linking with rel=noopener to external appointment system.
target_output.system_components: Hugo build process (Markdown + templates -> /public HTML). Netlify CI (build trigger, deploy-preview per PR, production deploy on main push). Netlify CDN (global edge distribution of /public assets). Decap CMS SPA at /admin/ (Git-backed content editor, Netlify Identity OAuth). GitHub repository (Hugo source, Markdown content, netlify.toml). Netlify Analytics (server-side page view counting). Netlify Identity (editor authentication for CMS, separate from public site).
target_output.workflows: Content Update by Staff: Non-technical staff member navigates to https://domain/admin/, logs in via Netlify Identity, selects the Services collection, edits the eligibility criteria for the asylum claims service page, previews the rendered output in the CMS preview panel, and publishes. Decap CMS commits the updated Markdown file to the main branch on GitHub. Netlify CI detects the push, runs hugo --minify in under 10 seconds, and deploys the updated HTML to the CDN. The live page reflects the edit immediately after deploy. Deploy Preview for Review: A staff member edits a draft news article and opens a pull request on GitHub. Netlify CI generates a deploy-preview URL for the PR. A second staff member reviews the article at the preview URL and approves the PR. On merge to main, the production site is updated automatically.
target_output.data_model: No database. Repository content structure: content/services/{slug}.md (front matter: title, description, eligibility_criteria list, languages_served list, booking_url, weight for nav ordering). content/news/{yyyy-mm-dd-slug}.md (front matter: title, date, summary; body: article text). content/staff/{slug}.md (front matter: name, languages_spoken list, specialization, photo relative path). static/admin/config.yml defines Decap CMS collections mapping each content type to its front matter fields and file path pattern. All content stored as UTF-8 Markdown in the GitHub repository with no external storage.
target_output.api_design: No API. The site has no dynamic endpoints. Appointment booking links are static href attributes pointing to the external appointment system URL. Decap CMS uses the GitHub API internally for content commits but this is transparent to the public site.
target_output.security_and_compliance: netlify.toml [[headers]] block for all pages: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data:; frame-ancestors 'none'. X-Frame-Options: DENY. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(). HTTPS enforced by Netlify; HTTP redirected automatically. No third-party scripts or tracking pixels included. Appointment booking external links include rel=noopener. Decap CMS /admin/ protected by Netlify Identity; public site has no auth surface. GDPR: no personal data collected; privacy notice page documents zero data collection policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Netlify CI triggered on every GitHub push: runs hugo --minify with Hugo binary pinned to 0.123 via HUGO_VERSION in netlify.toml. Publish directory: public. Build time typically 5-8 seconds. Every pull request generates a unique deploy-preview URL. Merge to main triggers production deploy. Rollback: Netlify dashboard allows one-click rollback to any previous successful deploy. No server infrastructure to manage. Decap CMS updates trigger builds via GitHub webhooks automatically. Netlify Slack notification configured for build success and failure events.
target_output.observability: Netlify Analytics shows daily page views and top pages by traffic (server-side counting, no client script, no IP storage). Netlify build notifications sent to Slack for deploy success or failure. No error monitoring or application performance tooling needed for a static site.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Netlify free tier: 100GB bandwidth/month and 300 build minutes/month — sufficient for under 1000 visitors/day and under 100 monthly content edits. Annual cost is domain registration only (~$15/year). Scaling: if traffic exceeds free tier, Netlify Starter at $19/month adds bandwidth and build minutes. CDN scales automatically with no infrastructure decisions required.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Core Site (2 weeks): Hugo project setup with Tailwind, page templates for services/eligibility/staff/news, Decap CMS configuration for all content types, netlify.toml with security headers, initial content migration from old site, deploy to Netlify with custom domain and TLS. Phase 2 - Staff Training and Handoff (1 week): CMS editor walkthrough for 2-3 staff members, written editing guide for adding news articles and updating service pages, deploy-preview workflow demonstrated. Phase 3 - Post-Launch (ongoing): Multilingual content structure in Hugo i18n for Somali, Arabic, and Tigrinya when translations are available; appointment booking form integration when form backend is provisioned.
target_output.development_guidelines: Hugo content archetypes defined for service_page, news_article, and staff_profile content types so new content files are pre-populated with correct front matter fields. Tailwind CSS configuration in tailwind.config.js with content glob for Hugo template files to enable class purging. No JavaScript in Hugo templates except the Decap CMS SPA at /admin/. External links in templates use a Hugo partial that enforces rel=noopener and target=_blank. netlify.toml is the single source of truth for build config and security headers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Non-technical staff inadvertently break site formatting through incorrect Markdown or front matter syntax. Mitigation: Decap CMS provides a validated form-based editor that prevents malformed front matter; build failure notification to Slack alerts if a commit causes a Hugo build error. Risk: External appointment booking system changes its URL. Mitigation: booking_url stored as a front matter field in each service page, allowing staff to update it via CMS without a developer. Tradeoff: Hugo over a JS framework chosen for simpler toolchain, faster builds, and no Node.js dependency for a team with no JS expertise.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Decap CMS over alternatives for Git-backed editing without external CMS database. Netlify free tier confirmed sufficient for expected traffic volume. Hugo chosen over Eleventy for simpler template syntax suited to non-engineering team maintenance. Multilingual support deferred to post-launch when community translations are available.

metadata.notes: Minimal static site for legal aid organization serving asylum seekers; Hugo with Decap CMS on Netlify free tier.

project_goal.value: Python SDK to help humanitarian tech teams migrate legacy beneficiary registration systems from proprietary XML-based data formats to the OpenHIE-compliant FHIR R4 standard, with built-in field mapping, validation, and transformation utilities
project_goal.rationale: Multiple NGO tech teams face the same migration problem independently; a shared SDK reduces duplication and ensures FHIR compliance

target_users.value: Python developers on humanitarian tech teams performing legacy data migrations to FHIR R4
target_users.rationale: Developer SDK consumed programmatically; primary users are engineers, not end users

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a PyPI package with importable API surface; no hosted service or browser UI

capabilities.rationale: SDK provides data transformation utilities, FHIR R4 integration, and field mapping; no frontend or auth capabilities needed in a library

complexity_level.rationale: FHIR R4 resource modeling and configurable field mapping with validation rules add complexity above simple data conversion

risk_level.rationale: SDK transforms personal beneficiary data; incorrect transformations could corrupt health records or violate privacy rules

data_sensitivity.rationale: Beneficiary registration records contain names, dates of birth, addresses, and household composition — personal data under GDPR

external_exposure.rationale: SDK runs within developer environments and migration scripts; no networked service exposure

access_model.value: None — library SDK has no authentication layer; consuming applications are responsible for their own access controls
access_model.rationale: PyPI packages do not implement access control; SDK is a pure computation library

feature_scope.value: Field mapping definition via YAML/JSON config files, XML source record parsing, FHIR R4 resource type mapping (Patient, RelatedPerson, Observation), validation against FHIR R4 constraints, batch transformation with structured error reporting, migration report generation
feature_scope.rationale: Complete migration workflow from legacy XML ingestion through FHIR bundle output

mvp_scope.value: YAML mapping config loader, XML source parser, FHIR R4 Patient resource builder with validation, batch transform producing FHIR Bundle JSON, structured error report output
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum set to complete beneficiary registration record migrations for pilot organizations

security_baseline.value: No network calls in core SDK; optional PII redaction utility for generating anonymized test fixtures from real source records; no logging of PII in default SDK logging output
security_baseline.rationale: SDK runs in developer environments where logging PII would create exposure risk in log files and CI systems

frontend_stack.value: None — library SDK has no UI; documentation via Sphinx/ReadTheDocs
frontend_stack.rationale: library_sdk project class; pure Python package with no browser interface

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with fhir.resources 7.x for FHIR R4 Pydantic models and lxml for XML parsing
backend_stack.rationale: fhir.resources provides validated Pydantic v2 models for all FHIR R4 resource types; lxml for high-performance XML parsing of legacy source files

data_platform.value: None — SDK reads from caller-provided file handles or strings and writes to caller-provided output streams; no embedded database
data_platform.rationale: Library SDK must not prescribe storage backend; consuming applications own their data persistence layer

hosting_target.value: PyPI package; GitHub repository with GitHub Actions for CI and automated PyPI release
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python library distribution via PyPI enables installation via pip/uv in any environment

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK itself retains no data; documentation instructs consuming applications to implement GDPR-compliant retention for source records and migration artifacts; PII redaction utility for test fixture generation
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK is a computation library with no persistence; data stewardship obligations fall on consuming application operators

future_scope.value: FHIR R4 Questionnaire and QuestionnaireResponse resource support for intake form migration; async batch processing for large datasets via asyncio; FHIR server direct upload client as optional extension
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 additions to cover broader FHIR resource types used in humanitarian health programs

constraints.value: Python 3.10+ compatibility; zero mandatory external service dependencies in core SDK; pip-installable with no C extension compilation required for core features
constraints.rationale: Must work in restricted environments where internet access and C compilers are unavailable; lxml is an optional accelerator, not a mandatory dependency

observability_baseline.value: Python standard logging integration; structured migration report dict output per batch; optional verbose mode logging per-record transformation steps without PII values
observability_baseline.rationale: SDK must integrate with whatever logging infrastructure the consuming application uses; no external telemetry in core SDK

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with GitHub Actions automated release pipeline; test-driven development with 90% line coverage gate
execution_preference.rationale: Public library requires strict versioning and high test coverage to signal reliability to consuming teams

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) must be documentable in migration artifacts; HL7 FHIR R4 specification compliance required for FHIR resource output validity; OpenHIE architecture compliance for interoperability with national health information systems
compliance_context.rationale: Migrated data entered into national health systems must comply with FHIR R4 and OpenHIE standards; GDPR applies to personal beneficiary data processed during migration

project.goal: Python SDK for FHIR R4 migration of legacy XML beneficiary registration records
project.target_users: Python developers on NGO tech teams performing data migrations

backend.framework: fhir.resources 7.x (Pydantic v2 FHIR models), lxml (optional XML parser), PyYAML for mapping configs
backend.api: Public Python API: FHIRMigrator class, MappingConfig loader, FHIRBundleBuilder, MigrationReport

security.auth: None — library SDK
security.pii_handling: No PII in SDK logs; PII redaction utility for test fixture generation

data.input: Caller-provided XML file handles or strings
data.output: FHIR Bundle JSON or fhir.resources Python objects

devops.ci: GitHub Actions (pytest, mypy, flake8, coverage gate 90%)
devops.hosting: PyPI via GitHub Actions release workflow

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK scope is well-defined for MVP: YAML mapping config, XML parsing, FHIR R4 Patient/RelatedPerson/Observation builders, validation, batch transform, and error reporting cover the complete migration workflow that NGO tech teams need. The PII redaction utility for test fixtures is a valuable addition that reduces risk of real data appearing in test suites.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using fhir.resources 7.x (Pydantic v2) as the FHIR model layer is the right choice: it provides validated resource construction with FHIR constraint checking out of the box. The XML parsing strategy needs careful design — the YAML mapping config must support XPath expressions for flexible element extraction rather than fixed tag name assumptions, as legacy XML schemas vary significantly across organizations.
reasoner_reviews.security: The no-PII-in-logs constraint is critical and easy to violate accidentally. The SDK must use a structured logging approach where field values are redacted by default and only field names and transformation operation names are logged. The PII redaction utility for test fixtures should use a format-preserving approach so test records look realistic without containing real names or birthdates.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero mandatory C extension requirement means lxml must be an optional accelerator behind an interface. The core XML parsing must work with Python's built-in xml.etree.ElementTree. The constraint for pip-installability in restricted environments means the dependency tree should be minimal: fhir.resources, PyYAML, and jsonschema as the only mandatory runtime dependencies.

backend.service_design: SDK public API surface: fhir_migrate.MappingConfig (loads YAML mapping definition, validates against JSON schema), fhir_migrate.XMLSourceParser (parses legacy XML using configured XPath expressions via xml.etree.ElementTree or lxml if available), fhir_migrate.FHIRResourceBuilder (constructs fhir.resources Patient/RelatedPerson/Observation objects from extracted field values), fhir_migrate.FHIRMigrator (orchestrates batch transformation, collects ValidationError instances, returns MigrationReport), fhir_migrate.MigrationReport (structured result: total_records, success_count, error_count, error_details list), fhir_migrate.PIIRedactor (utility class for generating anonymized test fixtures)
backend.api_patterns: Builder pattern for FHIRResourceBuilder: .set_field(fhir_path, value) calls mapped to fhir.resources attribute assignment with type coercion. Iterator protocol on FHIRMigrator for memory-efficient batch processing of large XML collections. MappingConfig uses JSON schema validation (jsonschema library) on load to surface mapping definition errors early.
backend.business_modules: FieldTypeCoercer: converts source XML string values to FHIR-expected types (date parsing for birthDate, HumanName parsing for name fields, CodeableConcept construction for coded fields). FHIRConstraintValidator: wraps fhir.resources Pydantic validation and translates ValidationError into structured MigrationError objects with source_record_id, fhir_field_path, and fhir_constraint_violated fields.
backend.failure_handling: Batch transform runs in two modes: fail_fast (raises MigrationError on first invalid record) and collect_errors (continues transformation, collects all errors into MigrationReport.error_details). Source records that fail validation are written to caller-provided rejection_stream if provided.

security.privacy_controls: Default logging level for field values is NEVER_LOG; SDK logger emits field_name and operation but not field values. PIIRedactor.anonymize(source_record) generates realistic fake values: random names from static word list, shifted birthdates (±30-365 days), anonymized IDs while preserving household structure for valid test scenarios.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: MigrationReport includes source_record_id for each error, enabling consuming applications to log which source records had mapping failures without logging PII values. No telemetry calls in SDK core — no HTTP calls, no file writes outside caller-provided streams.

data.entities: MappingConfig (source_resource_type, target_fhir_resource, field_mappings: List[FieldMapping], required_fields: List[str], transformation_rules: Dict). FieldMapping (source_xpath: str, target_fhir_path: str, field_type: Enum str/date/codeable_concept/human_name/identifier, required: bool, transformation_fn: Optional[str]). MigrationError (source_record_id: str, source_xpath: str, target_fhir_path: str, error_type: Enum missing_required/type_coercion/fhir_constraint, detail: str). MigrationReport (total_records: int, success_count: int, error_count: int, error_details: List[MigrationError], generated_at: datetime).
data.schema_notes: YAML mapping config format validated against a bundled JSON schema (fhir_migrate/schemas/mapping_config.schema.json) using jsonschema on MappingConfig.load(). FHIR Bundle output uses FHIR R4 Bundle.type = 'transaction' with entry.request.method = 'PUT' for idempotent FHIR server uploads.
data.migration_notes: SDK includes sample mapping configs for common legacy formats: UN UNICEF CPIMS+ XML schema, UNHCR proGres registration XML, and generic flat-element XML. These serve as starting templates for consuming teams.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package (fhir-migrate) published via GitHub Actions release workflow triggered on semver tags. GitHub repository with branch protection: main requires CI pass and 1 review. ReadTheDocs integration for auto-built documentation from Sphinx docstrings.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: matrix test on Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12; pytest with 90% line coverage gate; mypy strict type checking; flake8 + black formatting check; safety check for known CVEs in dependencies. Release workflow: tag v{semver} triggers build, twine upload to PyPI, ReadTheDocs build trigger.
devops.backup_and_recovery: Git repository is the source of truth; PyPI releases are immutable. Semver guarantees: patch releases are backward compatible, minor releases add functionality without breaking API, major releases document breaking changes in CHANGELOG.md.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a library_sdk project — the architecture is about public API surface design, not hosted infrastructure. Key decisions: fhir.resources 7.x provides Pydantic v2 FHIR models that handle constraint validation, eliminating the need to implement FHIR validation from scratch. The YAML mapping config must support XPath expressions for flexible legacy XML extraction. The no-PII-in-logs constraint requires a structured logging approach at the SDK design level. No FrontendAgent subplan since this is a library SDK with no browser UI. The plan quality target is moderate — the data_model and workflows sections will be solid but the observability section will be slightly thin.
target_output.title: FHIR R4 Migration SDK for Humanitarian Beneficiary Registration Data
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.10+ library SDK (pip install fhir-migrate) providing field-mapping-driven transformation of legacy proprietary XML beneficiary registration records to FHIR R4 Patient, RelatedPerson, and Observation bundles. Mapping configurations are defined in YAML with XPath source expressions and validated on load against a bundled JSON schema. The core transformation pipeline runs entirely in-process with no network dependencies, making it suitable for restricted field office environments. Structured MigrationReport output captures per-record validation errors with source_xpath and FHIR constraint details for developer remediation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Python library with no hosted service component. The SDK is organized as a pipeline: MappingConfig loads and validates YAML field mapping definitions; XMLSourceParser extracts field values from legacy XML using configured XPath expressions via xml.etree.ElementTree (mandatory) with optional lxml acceleration; FieldTypeCoercer converts extracted string values to FHIR-typed Python objects (date, HumanName, CodeableConcept, Identifier); FHIRResourceBuilder constructs fhir.resources Pydantic v2 model instances for FHIR R4 Patient, RelatedPerson, and Observation resources; FHIRConstraintValidator wraps Pydantic ValidationError output and maps to structured MigrationError objects; FHIRMigrator orchestrates batch iteration and collects results into a MigrationReport. The SDK exports no PII in default logging output — field values are never logged, only field names and transformation operation labels.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Python 3.12 (compatible 3.10+). FHIR models: fhir.resources 7.x (Pydantic v2 FHIR R4 resource definitions with built-in constraint validation). XML parsing: xml.etree.ElementTree (stdlib, mandatory) with lxml 5.x as optional accelerator if installed. Mapping config: PyYAML 6.x for YAML parsing; jsonschema 4.x for mapping config validation against bundled JSON schema. Type checking: mypy strict mode. Testing: pytest 8.x, pytest-cov for 90% coverage gate. Build/publish: GitHub Actions, twine for PyPI upload. Docs: Sphinx with Napoleon extension for NumPy-style docstrings, hosted on ReadTheDocs.
