previous_plan.title: Theory of Change Designer - Desktop Application Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A fully offline Tauri desktop application for NGO program managers to build and evaluate Theory of Change frameworks using a visual node-link diagram. Built with Vue 3 and Rust, persisting ToC models and monitoring evidence in a local SQLite database with CSV import support.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Tauri v2 desktop shell with a Vue 3 webview frontend communicating via IPC invoke commands to a Rust backend. All data stored in a local unencrypted SQLite file using rusqlite. No network connections; fully offline operation.
previous_plan.data_model: theory_of_change_models, toc_nodes (input/activity/output/outcome/impact), toc_links, outcome_indicators, monitoring_observations stored in unencrypted SQLite.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: OS file system permissions only; SQLite file stored in app data directory without encryption at rest.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for the ToC designer. ISS-001 (unencrypted SQLite) is the focus issue. Adding SQLCipher with passphrase management via tauri-plugin-keyring to the data, security, and deployment sections. Also addressing ISS-002 by adding automatic timestamped backup on save. The core Tauri + Vue 3 + Vueflow architecture is sound and does not need changes.

fix_report.action_taken: Added SQLCipher AES-256 encryption to the SQLite workspace file. Passphrase is generated on first launch via Rust rand::thread_rng, stored in the OS keychain via tauri-plugin-keyring, and retrieved transparently on subsequent opens. Updated data_model, security_and_compliance, and deployment_and_operations sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ToC workspace data encrypted at rest; laptop loss does not expose organizational strategy content

fix_report.action_taken: Added automatic timestamped backup: on each successful database write, a Rust backup command copies the SQLite file to a /backups subdirectory with a timestamp suffix, retaining the last 5 copies. Documented in deployment_and_operations and risks_and_tradeoffs.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Single-file corruption no longer causes total data loss; user can restore from the most recent timestamped backup

target_output.title: Theory of Change Designer - Desktop Application Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A fully offline Tauri v2 desktop application enabling NGO program managers and M&E officers to design Theory of Change frameworks as node-link diagrams, link strategic outcomes to monitoring indicators, import field data via CSV, and generate structured evidence gap reports at evaluation time. Built with Vue 3 and Vueflow on the frontend and Rust Tauri commands on the backend, persisting all ToC models in a SQLCipher-encrypted local SQLite database with automatic timestamped backup.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri v2 desktop shell wrapping a Vue 3 webview. All business logic handled by Rust Tauri commands exposed via invoke() IPC. The ToC graph model (nodes: input, activity, output, outcome, impact; directed links between them) is stored in a SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite 3 database in the OS app data directory. Monitoring data is imported from CSV or Excel files via the calamine Rust crate, parsed into monitoring_observations rows linked to outcome indicator nodes. No network connections or external services; the application is fully self-contained and air-gapped safe.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop shell: Tauri v2 (Rust). Frontend: Vue 3 with TypeScript, Vueflow 1.x for the ToC canvas, PrimeVue for form components, Pinia for state management, vitest for component tests. Backend: Rust with rusqlite (SQLCipher feature flag), calamine for CSV/Excel import, tauri-plugin-keyring for OS keychain passphrase storage, printpdf for PDF export. Build: GitHub Actions matrix on ubuntu-22.04 and macos-14, tauri-bundler producing signed .msi and .dmg installers.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ToC Visual Designer: drag-and-drop Vueflow canvas for creating and connecting input, activity, output, outcome, and impact nodes. Indicator Registry: attach named monitoring indicators with baseline and target values to any outcome or impact node. Monitoring Data Import: upload CSV or Excel files, map columns to indicators via a field-mapping wizard, validate and ingest rows to monitoring_observations with per-row error reporting. Evidence Gap Dashboard: matrix view showing each node's indicators against imported observations, color-coded by achievement status. Evaluation Report Generator: PDF summary of achievement, gaps, and recommendations per ToC node.
target_output.system_components: Tauri v2 runtime providing the native window and IPC bridge. Vue 3 webview renderer with Vueflow graph engine. Pinia stores: tocStore (nodes/links normalized map), indicatorStore, importStore. Rust command modules: toc_commands, indicator_commands, import_commands (calamine), report_commands, backup_commands. rusqlite with SQLCipher for encrypted local persistence. tauri-plugin-keyring for OS keychain passphrase management. tauri-plugin-dialog for native file picker. Backup module writing timestamped copies to /backups under the app data directory.
target_output.workflows: ToC Design Workflow: Program manager launches the app (passphrase retrieved from OS keychain silently), creates a new ToC model, drags node types from the palette onto the Vueflow canvas, connects nodes with directed links representing causal pathways, and labels each node. Evaluation Import Workflow: At reporting time, M&E officer selects 'Import Monitoring Data', picks a CSV export from DHIS2 or KoboToolbox, maps CSV columns to registered indicators via the field-mapping wizard, and the import_commands module ingests rows into monitoring_observations, returning a per-row rejection list for any validation failures. Evidence Review Workflow: Evaluation dashboard renders the evidence matrix; nodes with all targets met display green, partially met amber, nodes with no evidence red, enabling gap narrative authoring before PDF report export.
target_output.data_model: theory_of_change_models (id INTEGER PK, title TEXT, description TEXT, created_at TEXT, updated_at TEXT). toc_nodes (id INTEGER PK, model_id FK, node_type TEXT CHECK(node_type IN ('input','activity','output','outcome','impact')), label TEXT, notes TEXT, position_x REAL, position_y REAL). toc_links (id INTEGER PK, source_node_id FK, target_node_id FK, label TEXT). outcome_indicators (id INTEGER PK, node_id FK, indicator_name TEXT, unit TEXT, baseline_value REAL, target_value REAL, data_source TEXT). monitoring_data_imports (id INTEGER PK, indicator_id FK, file_name TEXT, imported_at TEXT, row_count INTEGER, error_count INTEGER). monitoring_observations (id INTEGER PK, import_id FK, indicator_id FK, period_label TEXT, observed_value REAL, notes TEXT). evaluation_reports (id INTEGER PK, model_id FK, generated_at TEXT, summary_json TEXT). All tables stored in SQLCipher AES-256 encrypted file at {app_data}/toc_workspace.db.
target_output.api_design: Tauri IPC commands: invoke('create_toc_model', {title, description}) -> ModelId. invoke('add_toc_node', {model_id, node_type, label, position_x, position_y}) -> NodeId. invoke('add_toc_link', {source_node_id, target_node_id, label}) -> LinkId. invoke('add_indicator', {node_id, indicator_name, unit, baseline_value, target_value}) -> IndicatorId. invoke('import_monitoring_csv', {file_path, column_mappings: [{csv_col, indicator_id, period_col}]}) -> ImportResult {imported_count, errors[{row, field, message}]}. invoke('get_evidence_matrix', {model_id}) -> EvidenceMatrix. invoke('generate_evaluation_report', {model_id}) -> ReportSummary. invoke('export_report_pdf', {model_id, output_path}) -> void. invoke('list_toc_models') -> Model[].
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher AES-256 encryption applied to the SQLite workspace file at rest. Passphrase generated via Rust rand::thread_rng on first launch and stored in the OS keychain via tauri-plugin-keyring (Keychain on macOS, Windows Credential Manager on Windows). On each launch, the passphrase is retrieved from the keychain and passed to rusqlite PRAGMA key before any queries. No network ports opened by the application. File system permissions restrict the app data directory to the current OS user account. No external telemetry or crash reporting services to protect organizational strategy confidentiality.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI runs on each push: cargo test for all Rust command modules, vue-tsc TypeScript validation, vitest for Vue component tests. On version tag push: matrix build using tauri-action on ubuntu-22.04 (cross-compiling Windows .msi via NSIS) and macos-14 (producing .dmg). Binaries signed with organizational code signing certificates stored as GitHub Actions secrets. Installers uploaded to GitHub Releases with auto-generated release notes from conventional commits. No auto-update server. Automatic backup: on each successful Rust database write, the backup_commands module copies toc_workspace.db to {app_data}/backups/toc_workspace_{ISO_timestamp}.db and prunes to the 5 most recent copies.
target_output.observability: Local structured log file at {app_data}/app.log written by the Rust tracing crate (INFO level default, ERROR for command failures). Log entries include Tauri command name, execution duration, and serialized error details. Users share the log file with support staff for issue diagnosis. Application startup logs Tauri version and SQLCipher version for compatibility debugging. No remote telemetry collection.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing infrastructure cost; entirely local desktop application. GitHub Actions free tier sufficient for CI builds (approximately 40 build-minutes per release). Code signing certificate approximately $200/year. Distribution via GitHub Releases at no cost. For future cloud sync feature, estimated $20-50/month on a lightweight managed PaaS.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): ToC canvas with Vueflow node and link creation, SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite persistence with OS keychain passphrase, indicator registration, basic CSV import with field-mapping wizard, evidence summary table view, automatic 5-copy timestamped backup, Windows and macOS installers via GitHub Actions. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Excel import via calamine, evaluation report PDF export via printpdf, improved evidence gap matrix with color-coded achievement status, multi-model management. Phase 3 (4 weeks): DHIS2 indicator feed optional online import, ToC template library for common humanitarian program archetypes, multi-model comparison view.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust: use thiserror for all command error types returned as serialized JSON to the frontend. All SQLite access through a single SqliteConnection wrapped in a Tauri-managed State<Mutex<Connection>>. Vue: strict TypeScript, component tests via vitest, Pinia stores with typed state interfaces. Conventional commits for automated changelog. Feature branches with PR review before merge to main. Tauri capability declarations in tauri.conf.json must be explicit; no wildcard capability grants.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OS keychain unavailable in some enterprise IT configurations. Mitigation: fall back to a user-set passphrase prompt on launch if keychain access is denied. Risk: Vueflow performance degrades with ToC models exceeding 100 nodes. Mitigation: implement canvas node virtualization and off-screen culling in Phase 2. Tradeoff: SQLCipher adds a Rust compile dependency and increases binary size by approximately 2MB; accepted given the security benefit. Tradeoff: manual update distribution via GitHub Releases avoids auto-update server complexity but requires users to actively check for new versions.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Vueflow over custom SVG-based graph for better event handling and maintained library support. Confirmed SQLCipher over application-layer field encryption for simpler rusqlite integration. Chose tauri-plugin-keyring for passphrase management to avoid hardcoded credentials. PDF export via printpdf Rust crate rather than browser print API for consistent cross-platform layout.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision addressing SQLCipher encryption and automatic backup for offline ToC designer desktop app.

project_goal.value: AI system that automatically triages and routes inbound humanitarian aid requests from affected populations to the correct cluster or service desk using NLP analysis of request content and urgency signals
project_goal.rationale: Reduces caseworker triage burden and accelerates response to urgent aid needs

target_users.value: Affected populations submitting aid requests, caseworkers reviewing AI triage, cluster coordinators managing service desk queues
target_users.rationale: Multi-role system with public submission and staff-facing review

project_class.rationale: Core value is NLP triage classification; AI pipeline is central, not peripheral

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, realtime, analytics, devops
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack AI system with real-time queue updates, model retraining, and DevOps modernization

complexity_level.rationale: LLM classification, human-in-loop correction feedback, real-time queue, and CI/CD pipeline complexity

risk_level.rationale: Processes personal data from vulnerable populations; AI routing errors could delay critical aid

data_sensitivity.rationale: Aid requests contain names, locations, household size, health and protection needs

external_exposure.rationale: Submission requires phone OTP; caseworker dashboard requires role login

access_model.value: RBAC with requestor, caseworker, cluster_coordinator, and admin roles; Azure AD for staff, OTP for affected persons
access_model.rationale: Differentiated access for vulnerable population submission and staff review

feature_scope.value: Aid request submission form, NLP triage classification, cluster routing, caseworker review dashboard, override and correction capture, model retraining pipeline, real-time queue notifications
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end triage lifecycle including human-in-loop feedback

mvp_scope.value: Aid request submission, LLM triage classification with cluster routing, caseworker review dashboard with override capability
mvp_scope.rationale: Core triage loop without retraining in MVP

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3, Azure AD for staff, OTP for submitters, RBAC, encrypted PHI fields, audit log on all AI classification decisions
security_baseline.rationale: Protects personal data of vulnerable populations and provides AI accountability trail

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; static build served via Azure Static Web Apps

backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem for LLM integration and ML pipeline tooling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for operational data, Redis 7 for real-time queue state
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for aid requests and triage history; Redis for live queue counts

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service
hosting_target.rationale: Organization standardized on Azure; AKS supports GPU node pool for model inference

privacy_retention_policy.value: Aid request personal data retained 3 years post-case closure; model training data anonymized before use; AI decision logs retained indefinitely for accountability
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational retention aligned with GDPR and organizational accountability requirements

future_scope.value: Multi-language triage support, proactive outreach to unmet needs, automated escalation to protection cluster for high-urgency cases
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap capabilities after MVP stabilization

constraints.value: Model must classify with >85% accuracy on held-out test set; AI decisions must be explainable and auditable; no external LLM API for requests containing personal data
constraints.rationale: Do No Harm principle and data protection require local model inference for personal data

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Application Insights for API metrics, MLflow for model tracking, custom metrics for triage accuracy drift
observability_baseline.rationale: AI system requires both infra observability and model performance monitoring

execution_preference.value: Iterative sprints; MVP in 10 weeks, DevOps modernization track running in parallel
execution_preference.rationale: Parallel workstreams for feature delivery and pipeline maturity

compliance_context.value: GDPR for personal data of affected populations, organizational AI ethics policy requiring human oversight and explainability, Do No Harm principle prohibiting automated denial of aid without caseworker review
compliance_context.rationale: Risk level high and personal data sensitivity require compliance context

llm_integration.value: Local fine-tuned multilingual BERT model for request classification deployed on AKS GPU node pool; no external LLM API permitted for requests containing personal data
llm_integration.rationale: Constraint prohibits external API for personal data; local model satisfies accuracy and data residency requirements

project.goal: AI-powered triage and routing of humanitarian aid requests using NLP classification
project.target_users: Affected populations, caseworkers, cluster coordinators

frontend.state: React Query + Zustand for queue real-time state

security.auth: Azure AD for staff, OTP for submitters

constraints.data_residency: No external LLM API for personal data

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 1 delivered the core triage classification loop but the DevOps pipeline for model retraining was not addressed. The caseworker override capture mechanism exists in the data model but is not yet wired into the retraining workflow. This is the primary gap for round 2.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The FastAPI + AKS architecture is sound. The unresolved issue is that correction events from caseworkers are stored but the pipeline for batching them into weekly retraining jobs on the GPU node pool is absent from the deployment topology. This needs explicit design before production.
reasoner_reviews.security: The constraint prohibiting external LLM API for personal data is addressed by the local BERT deployment, but the AI decision audit log lacks a tamper-evident storage mechanism. Appending to an unprotected PostgreSQL table is insufficient for accountability purposes.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The >85% accuracy target is achievable with the fine-tuned multilingual BERT approach, but there is no defined evaluation cadence or accuracy drift alerting connected to a monitoring alert threshold.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The real-time queue notification path using Redis pub/sub and WebSocket is architecturally correct but round 1 did not specify reconnection behavior for caseworkers on mobile devices in low-connectivity environments.

backend.service_design: FastAPI services: triage-api (request submission, classification invocation), caseworker-api (queue management, override capture), model-inference-service (BERT model serving via Triton Inference Server on AKS GPU pool).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful. POST /v1/requests for submission. GET /v1/queues/{cluster_id}/requests for caseworker queue. PATCH /v1/requests/{id}/triage for override. POST /v1/admin/retrain to trigger weekly retraining job.
backend.failure_handling: Classification service timeout falls back to manual queue with flag requires_manual_triage=true. Database write failures return 503 with idempotency key for client retry.
backend.background_jobs: Weekly retraining job: fetch correction events, anonymize personal fields, fine-tune BERT checkpoint, evaluate on held-out set, promote if accuracy >85%, update MLflow model registry.

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with two distinct surfaces: public-facing aid request submission form (minimal, multilingual) and authenticated caseworker dashboard (role-gated via Azure AD).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Submission: language selection, request form with request_type selector, urgency indicators, OTP verification, confirmation with case reference. Dashboard: queue view by cluster, triage result with confidence score, override modal with correction capture, analytics panel.
frontend.state_management: React Query for queue polling and request fetch. Zustand for WebSocket connection state and unread queue counts. WebSocket reconnect with exponential backoff for low-connectivity caseworker environments.

security.auth_design: Azure AD OIDC for caseworker and coordinator roles. Phone OTP via Azure Communication Services for affected person submission sessions. Short-lived JWT issued on OTP success with no PII in token claims.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced at FastAPI dependency: requestor submits and views own case, caseworker reads cluster queue and writes overrides, cluster_coordinator views analytics and manages escalation rules, admin manages model configuration.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable audit log table with INSERT-only PostgreSQL role triage_audit_writer: every AI classification decision persisted with model_version, confidence_score, assigned_cluster, and caseworker_override flag. No UPDATE or DELETE granted on this table.

data.entities: aid_requests (id UUID PK, requestor_ref, request_text_encrypted, request_type ENUM food/shelter/health/protection/wash/other, urgency_tier ENUM critical/high/medium/low, submitted_at, status). triage_results (id UUID PK, request_id FK, model_version, predicted_cluster, confidence_score, assigned_cluster, caseworker_override BOOL, correction_cluster, created_at). correction_events (id UUID PK, triage_result_id FK, caseworker_id, original_cluster, corrected_cluster, recorded_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all operational entities. Redis 7 for per-cluster queue depth counters and WebSocket pub/sub channels. Azure Blob Storage for anonymized correction event exports for retraining.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with system node pool (API services), GPU node pool (Triton Inference Server), and batch node pool (retraining CronJob, scales to 0 when idle). Azure Application Gateway for TLS termination and ingress.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline: pytest, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to AKS staging, smoke tests, manual approval, promote to production. Separate scheduled pipeline triggers weekly Kubernetes retraining Job.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor + Application Insights for API latency and error rates. MLflow tracking for model version history and accuracy. Custom metrics: triage_override_rate, classification_latency_ms, queue_depth_by_cluster.

ISS-001.title: Retraining pipeline not designed in deployment topology
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan stores correction events but provides no specification for the weekly retraining job infrastructure on AKS batch node pool or integration with MLflow model registry promotion.

history.note: Identified in architect_reasoner review: correction capture exists but retraining pipeline is absent.

ISS-002.title: AI decision audit log lacks tamper-evident storage control
ISS-002.detail: The round 1 plan writes AI classification decisions to a standard PostgreSQL table without INSERT-only role constraints, making the audit trail mutable.

history.note: Raised by security reviewer: accountability for AI routing decisions requires immutable audit trail.

focus_issues.title: AI decision audit log lacks tamper-evident storage control
focus_issues.detail: AI classification decisions persisted to standard PostgreSQL table without INSERT-only constraints.

latest_recommendations: Design the AKS batch node pool retraining CronJob with MLflow model registry promotion logic
Add INSERT-only PostgreSQL role for audit log table and document tamper-evidence mechanism

previous_audits.summary: Solid core triage classification architecture with React dashboard and local BERT deployment. Two significant gaps: DevOps retraining pipeline missing from deployment topology, and AI audit log lacks tamper-evident controls.
strengths: Local BERT deployment satisfies data residency constraint
Real-time queue with Redis pub/sub and WebSocket is architecturally correct
RBAC with Azure AD and OTP for affected persons is well-differentiated

Audit log table writable by standard DB role, not INSERT-only

previous_plan.title: AI Aid Request Triage System - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: An AI-powered triage system built with React 18 and FastAPI on Azure Kubernetes Service that classifies humanitarian aid requests using a local fine-tuned multilingual BERT model and routes them to the appropriate cluster service desk queue.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA communicates over HTTPS to FastAPI triage-api and caseworker-api on AKS. Triage API invokes Triton Inference Server on AKS GPU node pool for BERT classification. PostgreSQL 16 stores aid requests and triage results. Redis pub/sub drives real-time queue notifications to caseworker dashboard via WebSocket.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC for caseworker roles. Phone OTP for affected person submission. RBAC enforced at FastAPI dependency layer. AI classification decisions persisted to triage_results table for audit. TLS 1.3 on all endpoints.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster with system and GPU node pools. Azure DevOps CI/CD with Helm chart deployment. Azure Container Registry for images. Environments: dev, staging, production.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addressing two issues from the initial triage system plan. ISS-001 requires explicitly designing the AKS batch node pool retraining CronJob with MLflow registry promotion logic and an accuracy gate. ISS-002 requires adding an INSERT-only PostgreSQL role for the AI decision audit log to establish tamper-evident controls required by the organizational AI ethics policy.

fix_report.action_taken: Added AKS batch node pool specification: weekly Kubernetes CronJob fetches anonymized correction_events from PostgreSQL, runs BERT fine-tuning on GPU node, evaluates accuracy on held-out partition, promotes model version in MLflow registry only if accuracy gate >85% is met. Azure DevOps scheduled pipeline triggers the CronJob and monitors job completion.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Caseworker correction feedback feeds into model accuracy improvement on weekly cadence with gated promotion preventing accuracy regression in production

fix_report.action_taken: Specified INSERT-only PostgreSQL role triage_audit_writer with GRANT INSERT on triage_audit_log, no UPDATE or DELETE. Application DB connection for audit writes uses this restricted role. Documented tamper-evidence mechanism in security_and_compliance section.
fix_report.expected_outcome: AI decision audit log is append-only at the database permission level, satisfying accountability requirements for AI routing decisions affecting vulnerable populations

target_output.title: AI Aid Request Triage System - Revised Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An AI-powered humanitarian aid request triage system built with React 18 and FastAPI on Azure Kubernetes Service. A local fine-tuned multilingual BERT model deployed via Triton Inference Server classifies aid requests by type and urgency, routing them to cluster service desk queues in real time. Round 2 adds the AKS batch retraining pipeline with MLflow model registry gating and an INSERT-only audit log for AI decision accountability.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA with two surfaces -- public submission form for affected persons and authenticated caseworker dashboard -- communicates over HTTPS to FastAPI services on AKS behind Azure Application Gateway. Triage API invokes Triton Inference Server on a dedicated GPU node pool via gRPC for multilingual BERT classification. A weekly Kubernetes CronJob on the AKS batch node pool fetches anonymized correction events, runs fine-tuning, and conditionally promotes the new model version in the MLflow registry if the accuracy gate is met. All AI classification decisions are written to an append-only audit log via a restricted INSERT-only PostgreSQL role.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy. AI Inference: Triton Inference Server, multilingual BERT fine-tuned on humanitarian request corpus. Model Registry: MLflow on Azure Blob Storage. Database: PostgreSQL 16, Redis 7. Infrastructure: AKS with system/GPU/batch node pools, Azure Application Gateway, Azure Container Registry. Auth: Azure AD OIDC, Azure Communication Services OTP. CI/CD: Azure DevOps.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Aid Request Submission: multilingual form with OTP phone verification, request type and urgency capture, encrypted storage. NLP Triage Classification: Triton-served BERT assigns predicted_cluster, urgency_tier, and confidence_score per request. Caseworker Review Dashboard: per-cluster queue with real-time counts, triage result with confidence display, override modal capturing corrected_cluster. Model Retraining Pipeline: weekly CronJob processing correction events, BERT fine-tuning, accuracy-gated MLflow promotion. AI Accountability Audit: INSERT-only append log of every classification decision including model version and confidence.
target_output.system_components: Azure Application Gateway ingress for TLS termination, AKS system node pool running triage-api and caseworker-api FastAPI pods, AKS GPU node pool running Triton Inference Server with BERT model loaded, AKS batch node pool running weekly retraining CronJob scaling to zero between runs, PostgreSQL 16 for operational data with triage_audit_log on INSERT-only role, Redis 7 for queue counters and WebSocket pub/sub, MLflow tracking server on Azure Blob Storage, Azure AD for staff authentication, Azure Communication Services for OTP delivery.
target_output.workflows: Aid Request Triage: submitter completes OTP-verified multilingual form, triage-api encrypts request_text and invokes Triton gRPC for BERT classification, model returns predicted_cluster and confidence_score, request inserted into cluster queue, Redis queue counter incremented, caseworker notified via WebSocket. Caseworker Override: caseworker reviews triage result with confidence score, submits correction via PATCH /v1/requests/{id}/triage, correction_event persisted to PostgreSQL, audit log entry appended via INSERT-only role. Weekly Retraining: Azure DevOps scheduled pipeline triggers AKS CronJob on batch node pool, job selects correction_events from past 7 days, anonymizes personal identifiers, runs fine-tuning job, evaluates on held-out test partition, promotes model version in MLflow registry if accuracy exceeds 85% threshold, updates Triton model config map.
target_output.data_model: aid_requests (id UUID PK, requestor_ref VARCHAR, request_text_encrypted BYTEA, request_type ENUM food/shelter/health/protection/wash/other, urgency_tier ENUM critical/high/medium/low, submitted_at TIMESTAMP, status ENUM pending_triage/triaged/resolved). triage_results (id UUID PK, request_id FK, model_version VARCHAR, predicted_cluster VARCHAR, confidence_score NUMERIC(4,3), assigned_cluster VARCHAR, caseworker_override BOOL DEFAULT false, correction_cluster VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP). triage_audit_log (id UUID PK, triage_result_id FK, action VARCHAR, actor_id VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR, decision_cluster VARCHAR, confidence_score NUMERIC(4,3), created_at TIMESTAMP) -- INSERT-only via triage_audit_writer role, no UPDATE/DELETE. correction_events (id UUID PK, triage_result_id FK, caseworker_id VARCHAR, original_cluster VARCHAR, corrected_cluster VARCHAR, recorded_at TIMESTAMP). cluster_service_desks (id UUID PK, cluster_name VARCHAR, responsible_coordinator_id VARCHAR, active BOOL).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/requests body {request_type, request_text, urgency_signals[], otp_token} returns {request_id, case_reference, assigned_cluster, triage_confidence}. GET /v1/queues/{cluster_id}/requests?status=pending returns paginated triage queue with confidence scores. PATCH /v1/requests/{id}/triage body {corrected_cluster, correction_reason} records override and appends audit entry. GET /v1/requests/{id}/audit returns audit log for the request (admin/coordinator only). POST /v1/admin/retrain triggers manual retraining job. GET /v1/admin/model/status returns current MLflow registry version and accuracy.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC for caseworker and coordinator login. Phone OTP via Azure Communication Services for affected person sessions; JWT issued on OTP success contains no PII claims. RBAC enforced at FastAPI dependency layer per role. AI decision audit log uses INSERT-only PostgreSQL role triage_audit_writer: GRANT INSERT on triage_audit_log only, no UPDATE or DELETE, preventing post-hoc alteration of classification records. Request text encrypted at rest using Azure Key Vault-managed key. Correction events anonymized before export to batch node pool for retraining. GDPR-aligned: aid request personal data deleted 3 years post-case closure via scheduled job. Organizational AI ethics policy satisfied by caseworker override requirement before any aid denial and by append-only audit trail.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS cluster with three node pools: system (triage-api and caseworker-api, 2-4 Standard_D4s_v3 nodes), GPU (Triton Inference Server, 1-2 Standard_NC6s_v3 nodes with HPA scale-to-zero overnight), batch (retraining CronJob, scales to 0 between weekly runs). Azure Application Gateway handles TLS termination and ingress routing. Azure DevOps CI/CD pipeline: pytest unit and integration tests, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to AKS staging, smoke tests against staging, manual approval gate, promote to production. Weekly retraining pipeline: Azure DevOps scheduled run triggers Kubernetes Job on batch node pool, fetches anonymized correction exports from Azure Blob, runs fine-tuning script, logs experiment to MLflow, promotes model version if accuracy >85%, updates Triton model config map via kubectl. Rollback: Helm rollback to previous release; model rollback by reverting MLflow registry stage assignment.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor and Application Insights for API latency, error rates, and AKS pod health. MLflow tracking server records model version history, training accuracy per experiment, and retraining run durations. Custom Application Insights metrics: triage_override_rate (caseworker correction frequency as production accuracy proxy), classification_latency_ms (Triton gRPC p99), queue_depth_by_cluster (real-time pending count per cluster). Alert on triage_override_rate exceeding 20% over 24 hours, signaling model accuracy degradation and triggering emergency retraining review.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS system node pool Standard_D4s_v3 x2 approximately $280/month. GPU node pool Standard_NC6s_v3 x1 approximately $900/month with scale-to-zero overnight reducing GPU cost by ~60%. Batch node pool scales to zero between weekly runs. PostgreSQL Flexible Server GP_Standard_D4s_v3 approximately $180/month. Total approximately $1500/month. GPU node pool is the dominant cost; Azure Reserved Instances recommended if inference demand is predictable.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): aid request submission with OTP, BERT classification via Triton, caseworker dashboard with queue view and override, RBAC, INSERT-only audit log resolving ISS-002, PostgreSQL and Redis data layer, Azure DevOps CI/CD pipeline. Phase 2 (6 weeks): weekly retraining CronJob with MLflow accuracy-gated promotion resolving ISS-001, accuracy drift alerting, model version comparison in dashboard. Phase 3 (4 weeks): multi-language triage support beyond initial corpus languages, automated escalation rules for protection cluster, coordination leadership analytics panel.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints throughout FastAPI handlers. Pytest for unit tests covering classification pipeline, correction capture, and audit log insertion. Contract tests for Triton gRPC interface using saved request fixtures. React Query suspense boundaries for caseworker queue loading states. Azure DevOps pipeline fails on test coverage below 75% for API services. MLflow experiment naming convention: aid-triage-{YYYYMMDD}-{git_sha_short}.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: BERT model accuracy below 85% threshold on low-resource languages in multilingual corpus. Mitigation: weekly retraining on caseworker corrections focuses model on observed distribution shift. Risk: GPU node pool cost spike if Triton HPA misconfigured. Mitigation: conservative scale-up policy and scale-to-zero overnight. Tradeoff: local BERT over external LLM API sacrifices zero-shot multilingual breadth but satisfies data residency constraint. Tradeoff: weekly retraining cadence is a lagging feedback loop; high-urgency misclassifications may persist days before correction batch is applied.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed local fine-tuned BERT over external LLM API to satisfy data residency constraint for personal data. Chose Triton Inference Server for production model serving for throughput and model versioning. Selected Azure Communication Services OTP over TOTP to reduce friction for affected populations. Resolved INSERT-only audit log mechanism using PostgreSQL role-based access control.

metadata.notes: Round 2 fixes: AKS batch retraining pipeline with MLflow gating (ISS-001) and INSERT-only audit log for AI classification decisions (ISS-002).

profile.domain: Humanitarian Tech - Headless CMS Migration SDK

project_goal.value: A JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for migrating humanitarian organization websites from legacy CMS platforms to modern headless CMS architectures, with utilities for content mapping, asset migration, redirect rule generation, and SEO metadata preservation
project_goal.rationale: Reduces manual migration effort and error rate for NGO web teams with limited technical capacity

target_users.value: Web developers and technical staff at humanitarian organizations performing CMS platform migrations
target_users.rationale: Primary SDK consumers who run migrations

project_class.rationale: Distributed as an npm package with a programmatic API, no hosted platform

capabilities.rationale: Content extraction, transformation, and CMS API integration; no frontend or auth

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-CMS adapters and schema transformation logic add moderate complexity

risk_level.rationale: No sensitive data stored; processes public website content during migrations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes public website content pages and media assets only

external_exposure.rationale: Used by internal developer tooling, not exposed publicly at runtime

access_model.value: No authentication; CMS API keys provided by the user via config file or environment variables
access_model.rationale: SDK acts as a migration tool, not a hosted service

feature_scope.value: Source CMS adapters (WordPress, Drupal 9, Contentful), target CMS adapters (Contentful, Sanity, Strapi), content type mapping DSL, asset migration with CDN re-upload, redirect rule CSV export, SEO metadata mapping, validation report generation
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the full migration workflow

mvp_scope.value: WordPress to Contentful migration path: content extraction, field mapping, media upload, redirect export, and validation report
mvp_scope.rationale: Most common NGO migration path, delivers initial value quickly

security_baseline.value: CMS API credentials read from environment variables only, never logged or written to disk; HTTPS for all CMS API calls
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents credential leakage in migration scripts

frontend_stack.value: N/A - library SDK, no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK has no UI; consumers integrate it in their own scripts

backend_stack.rationale: Matches the JavaScript ecosystem of CMS tooling; TypeScript for type-safe mapper definitions

data_platform.value: No persistent database; in-memory processing with optional SQLite progress checkpoint for resumable migrations
data_platform.rationale: Migrations are one-time operations; progress checkpoints prevent re-processing on failure

hosting_target.value: npm package registry (npmjs.com)
hosting_target.rationale: Standard distribution channel for Node.js libraries

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by the SDK; all processing is in-memory or written to user-specified output paths
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK does not operate as a data processor for personal data

future_scope.value: Drupal to Sanity adapter, incremental migration support for live sites, CLI wrapper for non-developer staff
future_scope.rationale: Expand platform coverage and accessibility

constraints.value: No external service dependencies at install time; must work with Node.js 18 and 20; zero telemetry collection
constraints.rationale: NGO environments may have strict outbound network policies

observability_baseline.value: Structured console output with migration progress and error counts; optional verbose mode with per-record logging
observability_baseline.rationale: Sufficient for one-time migration runs

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with automated npm publish via GitHub Actions on version tag
execution_preference.rationale: Standard library release cadence

project.goal: TypeScript SDK for CMS-to-CMS website content migration with validation reporting
project.target_users: Web developers at NGOs migrating legacy CMS platforms

security.transport: HTTPS for all CMS API calls

data.persistence: optional SQLite progress checkpoint via better-sqlite3
data.formats: JSON for content, CSV for redirect rules, Markdown for reports

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: lint, typecheck, vitest unit tests, npm publish on tag

reasoner_reviews.product: The WordPress-to-Contentful migration path is the right MVP choice: it is by far the most common NGO stack transition and directly unblocks several teams. The content type mapping DSL is the key differentiator; without it, developers write bespoke transform scripts for every migration. Redirect CSV export is a practical touch since many NGOs rely on Netlify or Cloudflare redirect rules.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Pure TypeScript with no runtime dependencies beyond Node.js fetch is the right architecture for a migration SDK. The adapter pattern cleanly separates source extraction from target ingestion, allowing new CMS combinations to be added without touching the core transform pipeline. SQLite progress checkpointing is a strong addition for migrations of large sites that can fail mid-run.
reasoner_reviews.security: The main risk is CMS API credential leakage in logs or error messages. Credentials must be sourced only from environment variables and never appear in any log output. The SDK should implement a credential scrubber for error stack traces. HTTPS is enforced by the CMS APIs themselves but should be verified in the HTTP client configuration.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero install-time external service dependencies is achievable with native node-fetch in Node 18+ and optional better-sqlite3 for checkpointing. The constraint that the SDK works on Node 18 and 20 is straightforward to validate in CI with a test matrix.

backend.service_design: Core modules: SourceAdapter interface (extract: () => AsyncIterable<ContentRecord>), TargetAdapter interface (ingest: (record) => Promise<IngestResult>), MappingEngine (applies field mapping rules and transforms), MigrationRunner (orchestrates extraction, transformation, and ingestion with progress reporting), CheckpointStore (optional SQLite-backed resume state)
backend.api_patterns: Public SDK exports: MigrationRunner class, WordPressAdapter({apiUrl, credentials}), ContentfulAdapter({spaceId, accessToken, environment}), createMappingDefinition(schema), runMigration(source, target, mapping, options) -> MigrationReport
backend.failure_handling: Per-record errors are collected into a MigrationReport rather than aborting the run; records that fail ingestion are written to a rejection log file; CheckpointStore records successfully migrated record IDs so reruns skip completed items

data.entities: ContentRecord {id, content_type, fields: Record<string,unknown>, assets: AssetRef[]}, MappingDefinition {source_type, target_type, field_rules: FieldRule[]}, AssetRef {original_url, local_path, target_url}, RedirectRule {source_path, target_path, status_code}, MigrationReport {total, migrated, failed, skipped, errors: MigrationError[], redirect_rules: RedirectRule[], generated_at}, ValidationError {record_id, field, constraint, message}
data.schema_notes: ContentRecord uses a generic fields map to accommodate any CMS schema; type narrowing happens in the TargetAdapter. MappingDefinition DSL supports field rename, value transform functions, and conditional field inclusion.
data.migration_notes: Progress checkpoint stored in a local SQLite file (.migration_checkpoint.db) in the working directory; schema: migrated_ids (id TEXT PK, content_type TEXT, migrated_at TEXT). Deleted before a fresh migration run unless --resume flag is passed.

security.secrets_management: SDK reads CMS credentials exclusively from process.env; throws a descriptive error if called with undefined credentials. Log sanitizer strips any string matching the credential patterns from error messages and stack traces before output.
security.abuse_prevention: HTTP client enforces HTTPS-only URLs for CMS API connections; rejects plain HTTP targets with an explicit configuration error
security.security_risks: Risk of credentials appearing in error output if exceptions are rethrown by consumer code; mitigate with documented guidance to catch and sanitize errors before logging in CI pipelines

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR - npm ci, tsc --noEmit, eslint, vitest --run with Node 18 and 20 matrix. On version tag: semantic-release auto-increments version, generates changelog, publishes to npm with NODE_AUTH_TOKEN secret.
devops.environments: No deployment environments; npm package is the artifact. Test fixtures use mock CMS API responses to avoid live API calls in CI.
devops.devops_risks: npm publish failure on breaking API changes; mitigated by semver policy and integration tests with recorded API fixtures via msw (Mock Service Worker).

ISS-001.title: Credential leakage risk in error stack traces
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan did not include a credential scrubber for error output; CMS API tokens could appear in error messages logged by consumer CI pipelines.

history.note: Identified during security review

ISS-002.title: No resumable migration support for large sites
ISS-002.detail: Prior plan had no progress checkpointing; a failed mid-run migration would re-process all previously migrated content on retry, causing duplicate entries in the target CMS.

history.note: Identified during data review

focus_issues.title: Credential leakage risk in error stack traces
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan had no credential scrubber; API tokens risk appearing in consumer CI logs.

latest_recommendations: Add a credential scrubber that strips token patterns from all error messages before output
Implement SQLite-backed progress checkpointing with --resume flag to avoid duplicate ingestion on retry

convergence_state.reason: Credential safety gap in error output not yet addressed

previous_audits.summary: Clean adapter pattern architecture for the CMS migration SDK. Public API surface is well-typed. MigrationReport design is practical. Security around credential handling in error paths is insufficient.
strengths: Adapter pattern isolates source and target CMS specifics cleanly
MigrationReport with per-record error list is the right user-facing contract
TypeScript public API with typed MappingDefinition DSL is well-designed
concerns: No credential scrubbing in error messages; tokens could leak into CI logs
No resume capability for large sites; failed runs force full re-migration
blocking_issues: Credential leakage via error stack traces not addressed
recommendations: Implement a log sanitizer that replaces credential strings with [REDACTED] in all error output
Add SQLite checkpoint store with --resume flag

issue_updates.title: Credential leakage risk in error stack traces
issue_updates.detail: No credential scrubber found in security or backend sections

issue_updates.title: No resumable migration support
issue_updates.detail: No progress checkpointing described in data or backend sections

previous_plan.title: Headless CMS Migration SDK Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A TypeScript SDK published to npm that provides adapters for extracting content from WordPress and Drupal and ingesting it into Contentful or Sanity, with field mapping DSL, asset migration, and redirect rule export.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Adapter pattern: SourceAdapter extracts ContentRecords via CMS REST APIs; MappingEngine applies field transforms; TargetAdapter ingests to new CMS. No persistent storage; runs as a one-shot script.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: API credentials from environment variables. No scrubbing of credentials from error output documented.
previous_plan.data_model: ContentRecord, MappingDefinition, AssetRef, RedirectRule, MigrationReport in-memory objects; no checkpoint persistence.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for the CMS migration SDK. Focus issue ISS-001 is credential leakage in error output. Adding a CredentialScrubber utility that replaces registered token patterns with [REDACTED] in all error messages, integrated at the MigrationRunner and adapter error handling layers. Also addressing ISS-002 by documenting SQLite-backed CheckpointStore with --resume flag. Core adapter architecture is solid.

fix_report.action_taken: Added CredentialScrubber class that accepts registered credential strings and replaces them with [REDACTED] in any Error message or stack trace before the MigrationRunner emits error events or writes to the rejection log. Updated security_and_compliance and development_guidelines sections with credential handling guidance.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CMS API tokens no longer appear in rejection logs, console error output, or CI pipeline logs

fix_report.action_taken: Added CheckpointStore backed by better-sqlite3 with a --resume flag on the MigrationRunner. On each successful record ingestion, the record ID is written to .migration_checkpoint.db. On resume, already-migrated IDs are loaded and skipped. Documented in data_model and deployment_and_operations.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Failed mid-run migrations can be retried without duplicating already-ingested content in the target CMS

target_output.title: Headless CMS Migration SDK Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A TypeScript SDK published to npm that provides composable adapters for extracting content from WordPress and Drupal source CMSs and ingesting it into Contentful, Sanity, or Strapi targets. Features a typed field mapping DSL, asset migration with CDN re-upload, redirect rule CSV export, SEO metadata mapping, and a MigrationReport with per-record error tracking. Includes credential scrubbing for safe CI usage and SQLite-backed progress checkpointing for resumable large-site migrations.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure TypeScript library with no runtime service dependencies. Core pipeline: SourceAdapter (extracts ContentRecords via CMS REST APIs as an AsyncIterable), MappingEngine (applies field mapping rules and value transforms defined in the MappingDefinition DSL), TargetAdapter (ingests transformed records via target CMS Management API). MigrationRunner orchestrates the pipeline, emits progress events, collects per-record errors into MigrationReport, and writes failed records to a rejection log. CredentialScrubber sanitizes all error output. Optional CheckpointStore backed by SQLite records successfully migrated IDs for --resume support. HTTP calls use native node-fetch with HTTPS-only enforcement.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Node.js 18 and 20. Language: TypeScript 5.x with strict mode. HTTP: native node-fetch (Node 18+ built-in). Asset processing: sharp for image format normalization. Checkpoint persistence: better-sqlite3 (optional peer dependency). Testing: vitest with msw for mock CMS API responses. Linting: eslint with typescript-eslint. Build: tsc to dist/; dual CJS and ESM output. Publishing: semantic-release with GitHub Actions on version tags.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Content Extraction: WordPress adapter reads posts, pages, and custom post types via the WP REST API with pagination. Content Type Mapping DSL: TypeScript-typed definitions mapping source fields to target fields with optional value transform functions and conditional inclusion rules. Asset Migration: downloads media files from source CMS, optionally converts formats via sharp, and uploads to target CMS media library, returning new asset URLs for field substitution. Redirect Rules: generates a CSV of old-path to new-path rules for Netlify or Cloudflare redirect configuration. SEO Metadata: maps Yoast SEO meta fields to target CMS SEO schema. Validation Report: MigrationReport listing total, migrated, failed, and skipped counts with per-record ValidationError details.
target_output.system_components: SourceAdapter interface and implementations: WordPressAdapter (WP REST API v2), DrupalAdapter (Drupal JSON:API). TargetAdapter interface and implementations: ContentfulAdapter (Contentful Management API), SanityAdapter (Sanity HTTP API). MappingEngine: applies FieldRule[] to ContentRecord.fields, supports rename, transform function, and conditional rules. AssetMigrator: downloads, optionally converts, and re-uploads media. RedirectRuleExporter: compares slug changes and writes CSV. MigrationRunner: orchestrates pipeline with progress EventEmitter, collects errors, writes rejection JSONL log. CredentialScrubber: registers credential string patterns and replaces with [REDACTED] in all error output. CheckpointStore: better-sqlite3 table migrated_ids for --resume support.
target_output.workflows: Standard Migration Workflow: Developer installs SDK, creates a mapping definition file specifying source and target content type schemas, sets CMS API credentials as environment variables, and calls runMigration(source, target, mapping). MigrationRunner extracts ContentRecords from the source CMS in batches, applies MappingEngine transforms, uploads assets via AssetMigrator, ingests transformed records to the target CMS, writes any failed records to a rejection JSONL file, and returns a MigrationReport on completion. Resumable Migration Workflow: If a migration fails at record 500 of 2000, the developer re-runs with --resume flag; CheckpointStore loads the 500 already-migrated IDs, the SourceAdapter pagination skips or the MigrationRunner skips matching IDs, and the run continues from record 501 without creating duplicates in the target CMS.
target_output.data_model: ContentRecord: {id: string, content_type: string, slug: string, fields: Record<string, unknown>, assets: AssetRef[], source_url: string}. AssetRef: {original_url: string, local_path?: string, target_url?: string, mime_type: string}. MappingDefinition: {source_type: string, target_type: string, field_rules: FieldRule[]}. FieldRule: {source_field: string, target_field: string, transform?: (value: unknown) => unknown, condition?: (record: ContentRecord) => boolean}. RedirectRule: {source_path: string, target_path: string, status_code: 301 | 302}. MigrationReport: {total: number, migrated: number, failed: number, skipped: number, errors: MigrationError[], redirect_rules: RedirectRule[], generated_at: string}. CheckpointStore (SQLite): migrated_ids(id TEXT PK, content_type TEXT, migrated_at TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Public SDK exports: runMigration(source: SourceAdapter, target: TargetAdapter, mapping: MappingDefinition[], options?: MigrationOptions) -> Promise<MigrationReport>. WordPressAdapter({apiUrl: string, username: string, appPassword: string}). ContentfulAdapter({spaceId: string, accessToken: string, environment: string}). SanityAdapter({projectId: string, dataset: string, token: string}). createFieldMapping(sourceType, targetType, rules) -> MappingDefinition. MigrationOptions: {resume?: boolean, checkpointPath?: string, dryRun?: boolean, verbose?: boolean, concurrency?: number}. MigrationRunner emits events: 'progress' ({processed, total}), 'record_error' ({record_id, error}).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Credentials sourced exclusively from environment variables; the SDK throws a ConfigurationError if any credential argument is undefined or empty. CredentialScrubber is instantiated at MigrationRunner construction with the registered API tokens and applied to all error messages, rejection log entries, and event payloads before emission. The HTTP client enforces HTTPS-only target URLs, throwing a ConfigurationError for plain HTTP CMS endpoints. No telemetry or usage data collected. Documentation explicitly warns consumers not to log raw Error objects from the SDK to avoid inadvertent credential exposure.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: on pull request: npm ci, tsc --noEmit (Node 18 and 20 matrix), eslint, vitest --run with msw mock fixtures for WordPress and Contentful APIs. On version tag: semantic-release increments version following conventional commits, generates CHANGELOG.md, builds dist/ with dual CJS and ESM output, and publishes to npm using NODE_AUTH_TOKEN GitHub Actions secret. Package.json exports map specifies the ESM and CJS entry points. No deployment infrastructure required; the SDK is a local developer tool.
target_output.observability: MigrationRunner emits structured progress events: 'progress' ({processed: number, total: number, percent: number}) on each batch completion. Console output at INFO level shows batch counts and asset upload status. Verbose mode (--verbose) logs per-record field mapping decisions. MigrationReport written as JSON to the output path on completion. Rejection log written as JSONL to {output_dir}/rejected_{timestamp}.jsonl listing each failed ContentRecord with its MigrationError.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost; SDK runs entirely on the developer's machine. npm package hosting is free for public packages. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI for an open-source project. For large sites with 10,000+ content records, the CheckpointStore and async batched extraction prevent memory exhaustion; concurrency option (default 5) limits simultaneous CMS API calls to stay within rate limits.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): WordPressAdapter (posts, pages, custom types via WP REST API), ContentfulAdapter (Management API ingestion), MappingDefinition DSL with field rename and transform, AssetMigrator for media re-upload, RedirectRuleExporter CSV output, CredentialScrubber for safe error output, MigrationReport, vitest test suite with msw fixtures, npm publish via semantic-release. Phase 2 (4 weeks): DrupalAdapter (JSON:API), SanityAdapter, CheckpointStore with --resume flag, SEO metadata mapping for Yoast-to-Contentful. Phase 3 (4 weeks): CLI wrapper (npx cms-migrate) for non-developer staff, Strapi target adapter, dry-run mode reporting what would be migrated without making API calls.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess. All public API functions must have JSDoc with parameter descriptions and usage examples. vitest coverage gate at 80% for all modules. msw used for all CMS API call mocking in tests; no live API calls in CI. Adapter implementations must satisfy the SourceAdapter or TargetAdapter interface contracts validated by shared test suites. Conventional commits enforced via commitlint for semantic-release changelog generation. No default export; all exports are named.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CMS API rate limits causing migration failures on large sites. Mitigation: configurable concurrency option with exponential backoff retry in the HTTP client. Risk: breaking CMS API changes invalidating adapter implementations. Mitigation: adapter integration tests run against recorded msw fixtures pinned to specific API versions. Tradeoff: optional better-sqlite3 peer dependency for checkpointing adds a native module to some environments; documented as optional to avoid breaking pure Node installs. Tradeoff: supporting both CJS and ESM output doubles the build step but is required for compatibility with both Next.js and older Node scripts.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected msw over nock for API mocking to support both Node and browser environments in tests. Confirmed better-sqlite3 over sql.js for checkpoint store given the need for synchronous writes during migration. Chose semantic-release over manual version management for consistent npm publish workflow. Dual CJS/ESM output confirmed after testing with Contentful CLI tooling that requires CJS.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision adding credential scrubbing and SQLite-backed checkpoint store to the CMS migration SDK.

profile.domain: Humanitarian Tech Platform Engineering - Container Deployment SDK

project_goal.value: An open-source Go SDK providing standardized deployment helpers, health check utilities, and configuration management primitives for humanitarian tech teams containerizing legacy applications as part of a DevOps modernization initiative
project_goal.rationale: Reduces per-project boilerplate and inconsistent container health check implementations across 20+ legacy applications being containerized

target_users.value: Platform engineers and application developers at humanitarian organizations containerizing legacy Go and non-Go applications
target_users.rationale: Primary SDK consumers integrating it into their containerization efforts

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Go module, imported by application code, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Deployment configuration helpers, health probe HTTP handlers, and container orchestration integration utilities

complexity_level.rationale: Multiple sub-packages with different concerns require careful API design for ergonomic composability

risk_level.rationale: No sensitive data handled; SDK operates on configuration and infrastructure metadata

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK handles deployment configuration, not user or program data

external_exposure.rationale: Open-source SDK published to pkg.go.dev for public consumption

access_model.value: No authentication; open-source library imported via go get
access_model.rationale: Standard Go module distribution model

feature_scope.value: Environment schema validation with required/optional/typed fields, health check HTTP handlers (liveness and readiness) with customizable check functions, graceful shutdown helpers, rollout strategy helpers for Kubernetes deployments, secret reference resolution from env vars and mounted files, container manifest validation utilities
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the core concerns of containerizing a legacy application

mvp_scope.value: Environment schema validation, liveness and readiness HTTP handlers, graceful shutdown with configurable drain period, secret reference from env vars
mvp_scope.rationale: Core deployment safety primitives needed by all containerization projects

security_baseline.value: No secrets stored by the SDK; secret resolution reads from env vars or mounted file paths only; no outbound network calls from the SDK itself
security_baseline.rationale: SDK must be safe for use in air-gapped container environments

frontend_stack.value: N/A - Go library SDK, no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK has no UI; consumers import it into their applications

backend_stack.rationale: Idiomatic Go for infrastructure tooling; no external runtime dependencies

data_platform.value: No persistent storage; operates on in-memory configuration structs and environment variable reads
data_platform.rationale: SDK is stateless infrastructure tooling

hosting_target.value: Go module registry (pkg.go.dev) via GitHub repository
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Go module distribution via public GitHub repository tagged with semantic versions

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained; SDK does not collect or transmit any usage or configuration data
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Open-source library with zero telemetry by design

future_scope.value: Prometheus metrics integration for health check duration tracking, Kubernetes operator helpers, multi-cloud secret backend adapters (AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault)
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP observability and secret management integrations

constraints.value: Zero external dependencies in the core module (only Go standard library); SDK must work in air-gapped container environments with no outbound network access
constraints.rationale: Humanitarian deployments often run in restricted-network environments

observability_baseline.value: Structured log output via slog (Go 1.21+ standard library) for SDK-internal events; no external logging dependencies
observability_baseline.rationale: Consumer applications choose their own log sink; SDK emits to slog for easy integration

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with GitHub Actions releasing tagged versions to pkg.go.dev
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Go module release cadence

project.goal: Open-source Go SDK with container deployment helpers and health check utilities
project.target_users: Platform engineers containerizing legacy applications

security.secrets: env var and mounted file resolution only

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: go test ./..., go vet, staticcheck on Go 1.21 and 1.22 matrix
devops.release: goreleaser on version tag, pkg.go.dev auto-indexes from GitHub tags

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK addresses a genuine pain point: humanitarian tech teams containerizing 20+ legacy applications each implement ad-hoc health checks and config validation, leading to inconsistent Kubernetes probe configurations that cause deployment failures. A shared library reduces this surface. The graceful shutdown helper is especially valuable for applications handling in-flight requests during rolling deployments.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Zero external dependencies in the core module is the right constraint for an infrastructure SDK used in air-gapped humanitarian deployments. The sub-package structure (config, health, lifecycle, secrets, rollout) cleanly separates concerns and allows consumers to import only what they need. The health check handler pattern returning http.Handler composable types is idiomatic Go.
reasoner_reviews.security: The SDK's threat model is narrow: it must not introduce supply chain risk (no external dependencies) and must not inadvertently expose secrets in log output. slog structured logging should mask secret values when they pass through SDK log events. The secret reference resolver must explicitly refuse to log resolved secret values.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero external dependencies is achievable for the core module using only net/http for health check handlers, os for env var reads, and encoding/json for config serialization. Optional sub-packages (e.g., Prometheus metrics) can have dependencies behind build tags.

backend.service_design: Sub-packages: deploykit/config (EnvSchema definition, ValidateEnv() at startup), deploykit/health (LivenessHandler, ReadinessHandler composable http.Handler factories), deploykit/lifecycle (GracefulShutdown with drain period and signal handling), deploykit/secrets (SecretRef resolution from env vars and file mounts), deploykit/rollout (RolloutStrategy struct with MaxSurge/MaxUnavailable helpers for Kubernetes manifest generation)
backend.api_patterns: Idiomatic Go functional options pattern: health.NewReadinessHandler(health.WithCheck('db', dbPingFn), health.WithCheck('cache', cachePingFn)). config.MustValidateEnv(schema) panics at startup on missing required vars. lifecycle.NewGracefulShutdown(server, lifecycle.WithDrainPeriod(30*time.Second))
backend.failure_handling: config.ValidateEnv returns typed ConfigError listing all missing and malformed variables rather than failing on the first. health check functions returning errors cause the readiness endpoint to return HTTP 503 with a JSON body listing which checks failed.

security.secrets_management: SecretRef.Resolve() reads from os.Getenv or reads file at the mounted path; never logs the resolved value; panics with a descriptive error if the source is missing. SDK internal slog calls use slog.String with [REDACTED] for any argument named 'secret' or 'token' by convention.
security.abuse_prevention: Health check endpoints exposed by the SDK should be restricted to the Kubernetes probe network range by the consumer; SDK documentation includes a note recommending middleware to block external access to /healthz and /readyz.
security.security_risks: Risk of accidental secret logging if consumers pass secret values to SDK functions expecting non-sensitive strings; mitigated by typed SecretRef wrapper that prevents direct string passing.

data.entities: EnvSchema: {Fields: []EnvField{Name, Required bool, Type EnvFieldType, Default string, Description}}. ConfigError: {Missing []string, Malformed []ConfigFieldError}. HealthStatus: {Status string, Checks map[string]CheckResult, Timestamp time.Time}. SecretRef: {Source SecretSource (env|file), Key string}. RolloutStrategy: {MaxSurge, MaxUnavailable intstr.IntOrString, MinReadySeconds int}.
data.schema_notes: EnvFieldType enum: string, int, bool, duration, url. ValidateEnv coerces string env values to typed Go values and populates a Config struct via reflection or explicit field mapping.
data.migration_notes: SDK is additive; consumers migrate from ad-hoc health check routes by replacing their existing handler registrations with deploykit/health handlers, validated by matching the Kubernetes probe path configuration.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: go test ./... with -race flag, go vet ./..., staticcheck, govulncheck for dependency vulnerability scanning (even with zero deps, stdlib CVEs exist). Matrix on Go 1.21 and 1.22. On version tag: goreleaser creates GitHub Release, pkg.go.dev auto-indexes within minutes.
devops.environments: No deployment environments. Test environment: pure go test with no external services. Benchmark suite: go test -bench for health handler and config validation latency.
devops.devops_risks: Risk of pkg.go.dev indexing delays for new versions; mitigated by triggering GOPROXY fetch via GOPROXY.io webhook after tag. Risk of breaking changes in minor versions; mitigated by semantic versioning and Go module compatibility guarantee.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass architecture for an open-source Go SDK targeting humanitarian platform engineers containerizing legacy applications. Zero external dependency constraint drives the sub-package structure using only stdlib. Key design decisions: functional options pattern for composable health handlers, typed EnvSchema with ValidateEnv returning all errors at once, SecretRef wrapper preventing direct string passing. No compliance context needed for an infrastructure toolkit with no data sensitivity.
target_output.title: Container Deployment SDK for Humanitarian Tech - Go Module Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An open-source Go module published to pkg.go.dev providing platform engineers with standardized primitives for containerizing legacy applications: environment schema validation with typed coercion, composable liveness and readiness HTTP handlers compatible with Kubernetes probes, graceful shutdown with configurable drain periods, and secret reference resolution from environment variables or mounted files. Zero external runtime dependencies ensure air-gapped deployment safety.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Go library structured as a single module (github.com/org/deploykit) with five focused sub-packages: config (environment validation), health (probe HTTP handlers), lifecycle (graceful shutdown), secrets (secret reference resolution), and rollout (Kubernetes rollout strategy helpers). All sub-packages depend only on the Go standard library in their core implementations. Consumers import the sub-packages they need; there is no monolithic entry point. The SDK exposes no HTTP servers, databases, or external connections of its own - it provides composable building blocks that consumers wire into their application startup and server code.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 with Go 1.21 compatibility for standard library slog. Sub-packages: deploykit/config, deploykit/health, deploykit/lifecycle, deploykit/secrets, deploykit/rollout. Testing: go test with -race flag, testify/assert for assertions, httptest for health handler integration tests. Static analysis: go vet, staticcheck, govulncheck. Build: goreleaser for GitHub Release artifacts. Distribution: Go module registry (pkg.go.dev) auto-indexed from GitHub version tags.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Environment Schema Validation (deploykit/config): declare required and optional environment variables with types (string, int, bool, duration, url) and defaults; ValidateEnv() validates all at startup and returns a ConfigError listing every missing or malformed field. Health Probe Handlers (deploykit/health): LivenessHandler (always 200 if process is running) and ReadinessHandler (aggregates user-supplied check functions, returns 200 if all pass or 503 with JSON check results if any fail). Graceful Shutdown (deploykit/lifecycle): GracefulShutdown helper listens for SIGTERM/SIGINT, stops accepting new connections, waits for the configurable drain period, then calls server.Shutdown(). Secret Reference Resolution (deploykit/secrets): SecretRef resolves a named secret from os.Getenv or reads a file at a mounted path, returning a typed value without ever logging the resolved content. Rollout Strategy Helpers (deploykit/rollout): constructs Kubernetes RollingUpdateDeployment spec fragments with MaxSurge and MaxUnavailable from Go struct values.
target_output.system_components: deploykit/config: EnvSchema, EnvField (Name, Required, Type, Default, Description), ValidateEnv(schema EnvSchema, environ []string) -> (Config, ConfigError), MustValidateEnv panics on error for use in main(). deploykit/health: CheckFn type (func(ctx context.Context) error), LivenessHandler() http.Handler, ReadinessHandler(opts ...CheckOption) http.Handler, WithCheck(name string, fn CheckFn) CheckOption. deploykit/lifecycle: GracefulShutdown(server *http.Server, opts ...ShutdownOption), WithDrainPeriod(d time.Duration), WithLogger(l *slog.Logger). deploykit/secrets: SecretRef{Source: EnvSource|FileSource, Key: string}, Resolve() (string, error), MustResolve() string. deploykit/rollout: RolloutStrategy{MaxSurge, MaxUnavailable, MinReadySeconds}, ToKubernetesSpec() map[string]interface{}.
target_output.workflows: Application Startup Workflow: Platform engineer adds deploykit/config to their application; in main(), calls config.MustValidateEnv(schema) with the declared EnvSchema. On missing required variables, the process exits with a descriptive error listing all missing fields before any downstream connections are attempted - preventing partial startup failures in Kubernetes. Health Probe Registration Workflow: Engineer registers the SDK's ReadinessHandler on /readyz, passing WithCheck('postgres', dbPingFunc) and WithCheck('redis', redisPingFunc). Kubernetes readiness probe targets /readyz; when the database is unavailable, the probe returns 503 with a JSON body identifying which check failed, causing Kubernetes to stop routing traffic to the pod without restarting it. Graceful Shutdown Workflow: GracefulShutdown wraps the HTTP server; on SIGTERM from Kubernetes, the helper stops accepting new connections, waits up to 30 seconds for in-flight requests to complete, then calls server.Shutdown(ctx), allowing zero dropped requests during rolling deployments.
target_output.data_model: EnvSchema: {Fields []EnvField}. EnvField: {Name string, Required bool, Type EnvFieldType, Default string, Description string}. EnvFieldType: string constant enum (TypeString, TypeInt, TypeBool, TypeDuration, TypeURL). ConfigError: {Missing []string, Malformed []ConfigFieldError}. ConfigFieldError: {Field string, RawValue string, ExpectedType string, Err error}. HealthStatus: {Status string (ok|degraded), Checks map[string]CheckResult, Timestamp time.Time}. CheckResult: {Status string, Error string, Duration time.Duration}. SecretRef: {Source SecretSource, Key string}. SecretSource: EnvSource or FileSource. RolloutStrategy: {MaxSurge intstr.IntOrString, MaxUnavailable intstr.IntOrString, MinReadySeconds int32}.
target_output.api_design: deploykit/config: config.ValidateEnv(schema, os.Environ()) (map[string]interface{}, error). config.MustValidateEnv(schema, os.Environ()) map[string]interface{}. deploykit/health: health.LivenessHandler() http.Handler. health.NewReadinessHandler(...CheckOption) http.Handler. health.WithCheck(name, fn) CheckOption. deploykit/lifecycle: lifecycle.GracefulShutdown(srv *http.Server, ...ShutdownOption) error. lifecycle.WithDrainPeriod(d time.Duration) ShutdownOption. deploykit/secrets: secrets.NewSecretRef(source SecretSource, key string) SecretRef. (r SecretRef).Resolve(ctx context.Context) (string, error). deploykit/rollout: rollout.NewStrategy(maxSurge, maxUnavailable interface{}, minReadySeconds int) RolloutStrategy. (s RolloutStrategy).ToKubernetesSpec() map[string]interface{}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Zero external dependencies eliminate third-party supply chain risk. govulncheck scans Go stdlib CVEs in CI. SecretRef.Resolve() explicitly excludes the resolved secret value from all slog log events by design; the SDK logs the secret key name but never the value. Health check response bodies do not include environment variable names or secret key names in error details. The SDK's health handlers should be restricted to the Kubernetes probe network range by the consumer application; SDK README includes a code snippet showing net/http middleware to block non-cluster access to probe endpoints.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Go module distributed via GitHub public repository; pkg.go.dev auto-indexes when a semantic version tag (v0.1.0, v0.2.0) is pushed. GitHub Actions CI: on pull request: go test ./... -race, go vet ./..., staticcheck ./..., govulncheck ./... on Go 1.21 and 1.22 matrix build. On version tag: goreleaser builds release artifacts (source tarball, CHANGELOG entry from conventional commits), creates GitHub Release. GOPROXY.io webhook triggered post-tag to accelerate pkg.go.dev indexing. No application deployment environments.
target_output.observability: SDK-internal events emitted via slog at DEBUG level: EnvSchema validation summary (number of fields validated, any defaults applied), health check durations per check function, GracefulShutdown lifecycle state transitions (draining, shutdown_complete).
